questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Security hardening in CKAD context ensures non-root operation, restricted filesystem, and minimal privileges—critical in production.

- PodSecurityContext and container securityContext
- InitContainer for permissions setup
- readOnlyRootFilesystem and fsGroup
- Privilege escalation controls and capabilities drop

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: encrypted-processor
  namespace: secure-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      securityContext:
        fsGroup: 2000
      containers:
      - name: processor
        image: your-image
        securityContext:
          runAsUser: 100101
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          capabilities:
            drop: ['ALL']
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

## Code Example

```javascript
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: api-config
data:
  PORT: 3000
  APP_MODE: prod

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
template:
  metadata:
    labels:
      app: api
  spec:
    containers:
    - name: api
      image: my-api:1.0
      ports:
      - containerPort: 3000
      env:
      - name: PORT
        valueFrom:
          configMapKeyRef:
            name: api-config
            key: PORT
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 3000
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 10
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 3000
        initialDelaySeconds: 15
        periodSeconds: 20
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 200m
          memory: 256Mi

---
apiVersion: v1

metadata:
  name: api-service
spec:
  selector:

  ports:
  - port: 80
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills for multi-namespace automation, pod networking, and safe secret handling via Secrets and ConfigMaps.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob in a Namespace; PVC mounts
- InitContainer installation; jq usage
- Secret/ConfigMap usage; status tracking
- NetworkPolicy scoping; per-namespace egress control

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: metrics-watcher
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: collector
            image: registry.example/metrics-collector:latest
            volumeMounts:
            - name: metrics-pvc
              mountPath: /metrics
            - name: logs-pvc
              mountPath: /logs
            env:
            - name: ENDPOINT
              value: "https://metrics-portal:9090/api/v1/digest"
            - name: API_TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: metrics-secret
                  key: api-token
          initContainers:
          - name: install-tools
            image: appropriate/curl
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache jq && mkdir -p /logs"]
          volumes:
          - name: metrics-pvc
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: metrics-pvc
          - name: logs-pvc
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you rotate the API token without restarting the CronJob?
- What are trade-offs of using a CronJob for real-time metrics vs. a DaemonSet collector?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

questions.question: In namespace fintech, a Deployment 'gateway' with 3 replicas uses mTLS to backend services on port 8443. TLS certs are stored in a Secret 'gateway-tls' rotated daily. Patch a minimal manifest to: mount the Secret at /tls (readOnly); add an InitContainer to copy certs to /certs and write a PID file to /shared/gateway.pid; configure a sidecar 'tls-reloader' that watches /tls and signals the main process to reload TLS when changes occur; set readiness/liveness probes on /healthz; define resource requests/limits; add a PodDisruptionBudget requiring at least 2 healthy pods. Also describe how you would verify rolling certificate rotation under load and rollback?
questions.answer: Patch design: mount the TLS secret at /tls (readOnly); add an InitContainer to copy certs to /certs and write a PID file to /shared/gateway.pid; deploy a sidecar tls-reloader that watches /tls/current
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical patching of secret-driven TLS rotation, minimal no-downtime reload, and RBAC-friendly safeguards in CKAD scope. It emphasizes inter-container signaling, probe configuration, and availability during secret rotation.

## Key Concepts
- Secret mounting and readOnly
- InitContainer data preparation
- Sidecar for TLS rotation signaling
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- Resource requests/limits
- PodDisruptionBudget
- In-cluster secret rotation verification and rollback

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: gateway
  namespace: fintech
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: cert-init
        image: busybox:stable
        command: ["sh","-c","cp -r /tls/current/* /certs/ && echo ready > /shared/gateway.pid"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /tls
        - name: certs
          mountPath: /certs
        - name: shared
          mountPath: /shared
      containers:
      - name: gateway
        image: gateway:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8443
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /tls
        - name: shared
          mountPath: /shared
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8443
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8443
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "250m"
            memory: "256Mi"
      - name: tls-reloader
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh","-c","while true; do inotifywait -e modify,create,attrib /tls/current || true; if [ -f /shared/gateway.pid ]; then kill -USR1 $(cat /shared/gateway.pid); fi; done"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /tls
        - name: shared
          mountPath: /shared
      volumes:
      - name: tls
        secret:
          secretName: gateway-tls
      - name: certs
      - name: shared
        emptyDir: {}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test TLS reload without impacting traffic?
- What are failure scenarios during rotation and how would you mitigate them?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Deployment: gateway] --> B[Secret gateway-tls mounted at /tls]
  B --> C[InitContainer copies to /certs and ≤ writes /shared/gateway.pid]
  C --> D[Main container loads /tls/current TLS]
  D --> E[TLS reloader sidecar detects changes]
  E --> F[Main process reload via USR1]
  F --> G[Rolling restart checks and PodDisruptionBudget in place]

questions.question: Given a Deployment named 'image-processor' in namespace 'prod' with 6 replicas processing images from a Redis queue, design a practical patch to ensure graceful shutdown of in-flight tasks during rollouts, prevent simultaneous pod terminations, and maintain availability during node drains; include a minimal manifest patch adding a preStop script, terminationGracePeriodSeconds, readiness and liveness probes, and a PodDisruptionBudget targeting the deployment. What steps would you take to validate under load?
questions.answer: Patch deployment to add lifecycle preStop draining, a 90s terminationGracePeriodSeconds, readinessProbe /ready and livenessProbe /healthz, plus resource requests/limits; create PodDisruptionBudget wit
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical mastery of graceful rollouts, pod eviction safety, and CKAD patterns beyond basics.

## Key Concepts
- PreStop draining
- terminationGracePeriodSeconds
- Probes
- PodDisruptionBudget
- Node drains under load

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: image-processor
  namespace: prod
spec:
  replicas: 6
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: image-processor
        image: myimage
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command: ["/bin/sh","-c","/usr/local/bin/drain-tasks.sh"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 2
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
          timeoutSeconds: 2
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 90
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: image-processor-pdb
  namespace: prod
spec:
  minAvailable: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: image-processor
      tier: backend
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt for multiple queues or different backoff strategies?
- How would you monitor backlog during drains?

questions.question:
## CKAD Intermediate: Parallel Job with ConfigMap-driven work queue

In namespace batch, a CronJob schedules a Job named `image-processor` that consumes a list of image files from a ConfigMap mounted at `/config/files` and writes results to `/data`. Provide a minimal manifest patch to:

- run 25 pods in parallel and complete 500 tasks
- ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600; backoffLimit: 3
- InitContainer seeds `/work` from `/config/files`
- mount a Secret at `/secrets/api` for credentials
- securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true; capabilities.drop: ["ALL"]
- readinessProbe on `/healthz`, livenessProbe on `/readyz`
- annotate PodTemplate with `checksum/config` to trigger restarts on ConfigMap changes
- verification under load and rollback steps?
questions.answer: Patch CronJob to set spec.jobTemplate.spec.parallelism:25, completions:500, ttlSecondsAfterFinished:3600, backoffLimit:3; add InitContainer seeding /work from /config/files; mount config, data, and se
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to coordinate CronJobs, Jobs, InitContainers, and config-driven rollouts under load with strict security and lifecycle constraints.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob and Job parallelism/completions
- InitContainers for pre-work seeding
- ConfigMap/Secret mounts and config-driven restarts via checksum/config
- SecurityContext controls (runAsNonRoot, capabilities drop)
- Probes for liveness/readiness; TTLAfterFinished

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: image-processor
  namespace: batch
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      parallelism: 25
      completions: 500
      ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600
      backoffLimit: 3
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
            - name: seed-work
              image: busybox
              command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /config/files /work/"]
              volumeMounts:
                - name: work
                  mountPath: /work
          containers:
            - name: processor
              image: registry.example/image-processor:latest
              volumeMounts:
                - name: config
                  mountPath: /config
                - name: data
                  mountPath: /data
                - name: secrets
                  mountPath: /secrets/api
              readinessProbe: {"httpGet": {"path": "/healthz", "port": 8080}, "initialDelaySeconds": 5, "periodSeconds": 10}
              livenessProbe: {"httpGet": {"path": "/readyz", "port": 8080}, "initialDelaySeconds": 15, "periodSeconds": 20}
              securityContext:
                runAsNonRoot: true
                capabilities:
                  drop: ["ALL"]
          volumes:
            - name: config
              configMap:
                name: files-config
            - name: data
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: data-pvc
            - name: secrets
              secret:
                secretName: api-credentials
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          imagePullSecrets:
            - name: regcred
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify 500 tasks complete without SLA breach under load?
- How would you rollback if the ConfigMap change caused incompatibilities?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace backup workflow: a CronJob in each namespace named ns-backup that runs every 6 hours to back up a MySQL database running as a Pod in the same namespace. The Job should dump data with mysqldump using credentials from a Secret db-creds, compress to /backups, and store on a PVC mounted at /backups. Retain dumps per backup-config ConfigMap retentionDays, pruning old files. InitContainer installs mysqldump/gzip. Include readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the DB pod and the backup endpoints. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Implement a per-namespace CronJob named `ns-backup` that runs every 6 hours. Use an InitContainer to install mysqldump and gzip; mount a PVC at /backups; read database credentials from a Secret named `db-creds`; execute mysqldump to dump the database, compress with gzip, and write to /backups. Read retention policy from a ConfigMap named `backup-config` (key: retentionDays) and prune old backup files accordingly. Include readiness and liveness probes on the Job Pod. Apply a Namespace NetworkPolicy to restrict egress traffic to only the database Pod and backup endpoints.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests the ability to compose a production-ready backup workflow using core Kubernetes primitives (CronJob, Secret, ConfigMap, PVC, NetworkPolicy) while understanding scope, lifecycle management, and security boundaries.

## Key Concepts
- **CronJob per-namespace scope**: Each namespace runs its own backup Job, ensuring isolation and scope-bound retention
- **InitContainer**: Installs mysqldump and gzip tools at Pod startup before the backup container runs
- **Secrets and ConfigMaps**: Store database credentials securely and retention policies as configuration
- **PVC-backed storage**: Provides persistent storage for backup files independent of Pod lifecycle
- **Probes**: Readiness and liveness probes ensure backup reliability and Pod health
- **NetworkPolicy**: Restricts egress traffic at the namespace level to prevent data exfiltration

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: ns-backup
  namespace: my-namespace
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: debian:latest
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apt-get update && apt-get install -y mysql-client gzip"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: backup-volume
              mountPath: /backups
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: alpine:latest
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |
              mysqldump -h $DB_HOST -u $DB_USER -p$DB_PASSWORD $DB_NAME | gzip > /backups/$DATE.sql.gz
              find /backups -name "*.sql.gz" -mtime +$RETENTION_DAYS -delete
            env:
            - name: DB_HOST
              value: "localhost"
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-creds
                  key: username
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-creds
                  key: password
            - name: DB_NAME
              value: "appdb"
            - name: DATE
              value: "$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
            - name: RETENTION_DAYS
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: backup-config
                  key: retentionDays
            volumeMounts:
            - name: backup-volume
              mountPath: /backups
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["ls", "/backups"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["ls", "/backups"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          volumes:
          - name: backup-volume
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: backup-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Trade-offs
- **PVC vs. emptyDir**: PVC provides persistence across Pod restarts but requires pre-provisioned storage; emptyDir is simpler but loses data on Pod termination
- **InitContainer vs. multi-stage image**: InitContainer adds startup overhead; a custom multi-stage image is leaner but requires image maintenance
- **Per-namespace CronJob**: Simplifies scope and retention per namespace but increases operational overhead compared to a centralized cluster-wide solution
- **NetworkPolicy**: Enhances security but requires careful planning of allowed egress rules to avoid breaking legitimate traffic
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Namespace] --> B[ns-backup CronJob]
  B --> C[Mysql Backup Job]
  C --> D[Writes to /backups PVC]
  D --> E[Prunes using backup-config retentionDays]
  E --> F[NetworkPolicy restricts egress]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, set up a per-namespace resource-quota and limit-range for a dev namespace. Define HARD: pods: 5, requests.cpu: '1', requests.memory: '1Gi', limits.cpu: '2', limits.memory: '2Gi'. Add a LimitRange with min cpu: '100m', max: '500m', min memory: '128Mi', max: '512Mi'. Provide minimal YAML skeleton for the namespace, a ResourceQuota, a LimitRange, and a Deployment 'web' with two nginx replicas. Explain testing and trade-offs?
questions.answer: To solve, create a dev Namespace and enforce governance with a ResourceQuota HARD: pods: 5, requests.cpu: '1', requests.memory: '1Gi', limits.cpu: '2', limits.memory: '2Gi', plus a LimitRange with min
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of Kubernetes resource governance and multi-tenant fairness in CKAD context.

## Key Concepts
- ResourceQuota HARD and scoped quotas
- LimitRange min/max and defaults
- Namespace scoping and validation
- Troubleshooting quotas with kubectl events

## Code Example
```yaml
# Namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev

# ResourceQuota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-quota
spec:
  hard:
    pods: 5
    requests.cpu: '1'
    requests.memory: '1Gi'
    limits.cpu: '2'
    limits.memory: '2Gi'

# LimitRange
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: dev-limit
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: '500m'
      memory: '512Mi'
    min:
      cpu: '100m'
      memory: '128Mi'
    type: Container

# Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
  namespace: dev
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:latest
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: '200m'
            memory: '256Mi'
          limits:
            cpu: '300m'
            memory: '256Mi'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you alert on quota utilization changes?
- What happens if a user legitimately needs more capacity?

questions.question: In namespace payments, a Deployment 'processor' with 3 replicas reads daily CSV feeds from a ConfigMap mounted at /etc/processor and writes to a PVC. Propose a minimal manifest patch to add a sidecar container 'config-watcher' that watches /etc/processor and sends a SIGHUP to the main process when the ConfigMap changes, preserving the existing mount and rolling restart trigger. Include the patch and explain how you'd verify behavior under load and perform rollback?
questions.answer: Patch adds a sidecar container that watches /etc/processor using inotify-tools and sends SIGHUP to PID 1 when the ConfigMap changes. This preserves the existing ConfigMap mount and leverages the native ConfigMap change trigger for rolling restarts. The sidecar runs as a non-root process and uses a minimal busybox image to avoid increasing the deployment's resource footprint.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of the sidecar pattern for config reloading without full pod restarts.

## Key Concepts
- Sidecar container pattern
- inotify for file system monitoring
- SIGHUP for process signal handling
- ConfigMap mounting and reload behavior
- Zero-downtime configuration updates

## Patch Example
```yaml
# payments/processor-deployment.yaml
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: config-watcher
          image: busybox:stable
          command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              apk add --no-cache inotify-tools
              inotifywait -m -e modify -r /etc/processor |
              while read path event; do
                kill -HUP 1
              done
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /etc/processor
              readOnly: true
```

## Verification Under Load
1. Apply patch and verify pod rollout: `kubectl rollout status deployment/processor -n payments`
2. Simulate ConfigMap update: `kubectl create configmap processor-config --from-literal=data=updated --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -`
3. Verify sidecar detects change: `kubectl logs processor-<pod> -c config-watcher -n payments`
4. Confirm main process reloads: check application logs for reload indicators
5. Load test with tools like `hey` or `wrk` to verify zero downtime during reload

## Rollback Procedure
1. `kubectl rollout undo deployment/processor -n payments`
2. Verify previous version: `kubectl rollout status deployment/processor -n payments`
3. Confirm stable state: `kubectl get pods -n payments -l app=processor`

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Include TLS-sidecar, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton?
questions.answer: A per-namespace manifest-resource-checker: a Deployment tails /manifests, runs a Python script to verify that each container has resource.limits and resource.requests, writes per-manifest results to /
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests in-cluster file handling, simple Python scripting, and how to wire sidecars, init containers, and policies in a CKAD context.

## Key Concepts
- In-cluster file polling from a PVC
- YAML parsing with PyYAML; resource fields validation
- InitContainers, Secrets, and TLS sidecars
- Probes and NetworkPolicy

## Code Example
```python
# manifest_checker.py
import yaml
import json
import requests
# simplified check function
def ok(manifest):
  try:
    for c in manifest['spec']['template']['spec']['containers']:
      r = c.get('resources', {})
      if 'limits' not in r or 'requests' not in r:
        return False
    return True
  except Exception:
    return False
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale this across many namespaces?
- What are failure modes if manifests reference unknown images or pull secrets?

Tests practical CKAD orchestration across multiple resources: per-namespace autonomy, lifecycle cleanup, and secure external reporting. It evaluates understanding of CronJob scope, ConfigMaps for TTL, Secrets, and network isolation.

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
```

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace image-provenance audit: a ScheduleJob runs every 30m, lists pods, resolves image digests from a registry using a Secret REGISTRY_TOKEN, and writes a digest report to a PVC at /audit while updating a namespace ConfigMap audit-status with last_run and digest_count. InitContainer installs curl/jq; add readiness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy to allow registry egress. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace image-provenance audit: a CronJob runs every 30 minutes, lists pods, resolves image digests from a registry using a Secret REGISTRY_TOKEN, writes a digest report to a PVC at /audit, and updates a namespace ConfigMap audit-status with last_run and digest_count. InitContainer installs curl/jq; add readiness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy to allow registry egress. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests the ability to design per-namespace automation with auditability and tight network controls. Image provenance is a critical security concern for multi-tenant environments like Discord or Airbnb, where ensuring container images come from trusted registries is essential.

## Key Concepts
- **CKAD Scheduling**: CronJob for recurring audit tasks
- **Image Provenance**: Registry API integration, image digests (SHA256)
- **Secrets & ConfigMaps**: REGISTRY_TOKEN for authentication, audit-status for state tracking
- **PV-backed Storage**: PVC at /audit for persistent digest reports
- **InitContainers**: Installing required tools (curl, jq) for image inspection
- **Readiness Probes**: Ensuring the pod is ready before accepting traffic
- **Namespace NetworkPolicy**: Restricting egress to only trusted registries

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: image-provenance-audit
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  schedule: "*/30 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: audit-sa
          initContainers:
          - name: tools
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: audit-volume
              mountPath: /audit
          containers:
          - name: auditor
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh", "-c", "..."]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: audit-volume
              mountPath: /audit
            - name: registry-token
              mountPath: /secrets
              readOnly: true
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["sh", "-c", "[ -f /audit/ready ]"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
          volumes:
          - name: audit-volume
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: audit-pvc
          - name: registry-token
            secret:
              secretName: REGISTRY_TOKEN
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: audit-status
  namespace: target-namespace
data:
  last_run: ""
  digest_count: "0"
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: registry-egress
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: registry
```

## Trade-offs Discussion
- **CronJob vs Job**: CronJob provides scheduling but has slight timing drift; use precise timing if required.
- **PVC Size**: Allocate sufficient storage based on pod count and audit frequency.
- **InitContainer Overhead**: Adds startup time but ensures tools are available; consider using a custom image with pre-installed tools.
- **NetworkPolicy Scope**: A permissive egress may be necessary if multiple registries are used; balance security with operational flexibility.
- **ConfigMap Updates**: Frequent updates may cause contention; consider annotation-based status or external database for high-frequency audits.

  Registry --> Token[REGISTRY_TOKEN Secret]
  ReportWrites --> Status[Audit-status ConfigMap]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, implement a per-namespace image-tag-enforcer CronJob that runs every 5 minutes in a namespace to enforce a policy-config from a Namespace ConfigMap named policy-config. The policy defines imageRepo and imageTag that must match all container images in Deployments in that namespace. If a mismatch is found, patch the Deployment to update the image to imageRepo:imageTag, and write a per-run heartbeat log to a PVC mounted at /logs, and POST a summary to http://policy-portal:8080/api/v1/enforce. Use an InitContainer to install curl and jq; add readiness and liveness probes; a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to policy-portal. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Implement a per-namespace CronJob that runs every 5 minutes to enforce image tag policies. The job reads policy configuration from a namespace-scoped ConfigMap named `policy-config` (containing `imageRepo` and `imageTag` fields), identifies all Deployments in the namespace with mismatched container images, patches them to the correct `imageRepo:imageTag`, writes a heartbeat log to a PVC mounted at `/logs`, and POSTs an enforcement summary to `http://policy-portal:8080/api/v1/enforce`. Use an InitContainer to install `curl` and `jq`; add readiness and liveness probes to the CronJob's pods; and apply a namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to `policy-portal` only. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests practical CKAD skills, including permissioning, automation, and Kubernetes-native policy enforcement using a CronJob with ConfigMap-driven behavior.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped CronJob for periodic enforcement
- ConfigMap-driven policy configuration (imageRepo, imageTag)
- kubectl patch or server-side apply for image updates
- PVC-based logging and HTTP POST for audit trails
- InitContainer for tooling installation (curl, jq)
- Namespace NetworkPolicy scoping for egress control

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: image-tag-enforcer
  namespace: default
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: busybox
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl jq"]
          containers:
          - name: enforcer
            image: curlimages/curl:latest
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |
              # Read policy from ConfigMap
              # Get all Deployments in namespace
              # Compare images against policy
              # Patch mismatched Deployments
              # Write heartbeat log to /logs
              # POST summary to policy-portal
            volumeMounts:
            - name: log-volume
              mountPath: /logs
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["test", "-f", "/logs/ready"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["test", "-f", "/logs/alive"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          volumes:
          - name: log-volume
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: enforcer-logs
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-egress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: policy-portal
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
```

## Trade-offs
- **Frequency**: Running every 5 minutes balances responsiveness vs. resource usage; longer intervals reduce API load but delay remediation
- **PVC vs. Sidecar**: Using a PVC is simpler but requires storage provisioning; a sidecar could stream logs directly
- **Patch vs. Reconcile Controller**: A declarative controller would be more robust but requires custom operator development; CronJob is quicker to implement but imperative
- **InitContainer**: Pre-installing tools increases pod startup time; consider using a custom image with tools baked in instead
- **NetworkPolicy**: Restricting egress improves security but may break legitimate communication; ensure all required endpoints are explicitly allowed
- **Idempotency**: Multiple enforcers running simultaneously could cause conflicts; consider adding a lock mechanism or using `concurrencyPolicy: Forbid`

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design per-namespace automation for safe, auditable canary rollouts with minimal disruption. It emphasizes idempotence, config-driven behavior, and secure traffic controls.

- CronJob scope and schedule
- Namespace-scoped ConfigMaps and Secrets interplay

- NetworkPolicy least privilege in a per-namespace context

## Code Example
```javascript
# Minimal CronJob skeleton (yaml shown as JS block per guidelines)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: canary-roller
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: install-tools
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl jq kubectl"]
          containers:
          - name: canary-roller

            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: canary-logs
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical rollout skills: blue/green pattern, traffic routing, probes, and quick rollback under load.

- Blue/Green deployment
- Canary routing with minimal impact
- Resource requests/limits, readinessProbe, livenessProbe
- Service selector switching for traffic control

## Code Example

```yaml
# Deployment patch for canary
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-canary
  namespace: prod
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myorg/web-app:canary
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "300m"
            memory: "256Mi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
```

```yaml
# Service patched for blue/green traffic routing
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-app
  namespace: prod
spec:
  selector:
    app: web-app-blue
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
```

```yaml
# Canary Service (optional, if using separate service for canary routing)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-app-canary-svc
  namespace: prod
spec:
  selector:
    app: web-app-canary
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
```

- How would you automate switching traffic and rollback if issues appear?
- How would you validate stability under a 50k RPS load during the canary phase?

questions.question: In namespace ops, enforce per-namespace resource governance by adding a ResourceQuota named rq-ops with hard limits: requests.cpu 40, requests.memory 160Gi, limits.cpu 80, limits.memory 320Gi. Verify enforcement by deploying test workloads that exceed the quotas, then observe pod events for admission failures and check the ResourceQuota status. Rollback by deleting or patching the ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources, or by reverting to a previously saved manifest.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical governance of resources in a namespace, a common production constraint. It also probes how candidates apply quota and limit controls to real workloads.

## Key Concepts
- ResourceQuota and LimitRange interplay
- requests vs limits, scheduling behavior, and event inspection
- Rollback strategies and observability

## Code Example
```yaml
# ResourceQuota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-ops
  namespace: ops
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "40"
    requests.memory: "160Gi"
    limits.cpu: "80"
    limits.memory: "320Gi"
```
```yaml
# LimitRange
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: lr-ops
  namespace: ops
spec:
  limits:
  - defaultRequest:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 128Mi
    defaultLimit:
      cpu: 300m
      memory: 512Mi
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: 1Gi
    type: Container
```

flowchart TD
  A[Ops Namespace] --> B[ResourceQuota rq-ops]
  B --> C[LimitRange lr-ops]

  D --> E[Quota enforcement on scheduling]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: secure container runtime, init containers, config-driven rollouts, resource limits, probes, graceful termination, disruption budgets, and network isolation.

## Key Concepts
- SecurityContext and podSecurityContext for non-root operation
- InitContainer usage and seed data from ConfigMap
- Rolling restart triggers via checksum/config annotation
- Probes and termination settings
- PodDisruptionBudget and NetworkPolicy integration

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal patch skeleton (conceptual)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: worker
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        checksum/config: "<computed-from-configmap>"
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: myorg/worker:latest
          securityContext:
            runAsUser: 1000
            runAsGroup: 1000
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          env: []
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /data
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "256Mi"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 8080
      initContainers:
        - name: seed-data
          image: busybox
          command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /config/bootstrap/* /data/" ]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /data
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
      volumes:
        - name: data
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: worker-data-pvc
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: worker-config
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you automate the checksum calculation for the config annotation in CI/CD?
- How would you verify rollback behavior in a live cluster without affecting production traffic?

## Why This Is Asked

Real-world backup automation at namespace scope is common; it exercises InitContainers, Secrets, ConfigMaps, and volume management within CKAD confines.

## Key Concepts

- CronJob in a namespace
- PVC backups via Restic using Secrets
- ConfigMap-driven PVC enumeration
- InitContainers and readiness/liveness probes
- Namespace NetworkPolicy for egress control
- Logs stored on a PVC

## Code Example

```yaml
# minimal CronJob skeleton
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: ns-backup
  namespace: my-namespace
spec:
  schedule: 0 2 * * *
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: restic-installer
            image: alpine:3.17
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'apk add --no-cache restic']
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: alpine:3.17
            command: ['sh', '-c', '/usr/local/bin/backup.sh']
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            - name: pvcs
              mountPath: /config
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          - name: pvcs
            configMap:
              name: backup-pvcs
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: per-namespace scope, Pod image inspection, external API interaction, and secure data handling with Secrets, ConfigMaps, and PVCs.

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ns-security-baseline
  namespace: <target-namespace>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl jq || true"]
      containers:
      - name: baseline-scanner
        image: registry.example/scanner:latest
        env:
        - name: CVE_API_URL
          value: "https://cve-feed.example/api/v1/cves"
        - name: API_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: cve-token
              key: token
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: ns-logs
```

Patch Ingress to route 10% to canary; later 50% and 100%. Include minimal YAML patches for deployments and a canary Ingress with canary-weight; how would you monitor health and rollback?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a safe, observable canary rollout using standard Kubernetes resources and NGINX Ingress without a service mesh.

## Key Concepts
- Canary deployment pattern with separate Deployments
- Ingress annotations (nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary, canary-weight)
- Progressive traffic shifting and rollback strategy
- Observability: latency, error rate, saturation

## Code Example
```yaml
# Stable Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: frontend
  namespace: prod
  labels:
    app: frontend
    version: stable
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
      version: stable
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: frontend
        version: stable
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: frontend
        image: frontend:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```
```yaml
# Canary Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: frontend-canary
  namespace: prod
  labels:
    app: frontend
    version: canary
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
      version: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: frontend
        version: canary
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: frontend
        image: frontend:1.2-canary
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```
```yaml
# Ingress (production path)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: frontend-ingress
  namespace: prod
  annotations:
    # enable canary routing for the canary backend
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "10"
spec:
  rules:
  - host: "frontend.example.com"
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: frontend-canary
            port:
              number: 80
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you promote canary to 50% and then 100% safely?
- What metrics would you monitor to decide progression or rollback?
- How would you rollback if the canary fails without affecting stable users?

  B --> C[Stable Backend (frontend)]
  B --> D[Canary Route (frontend-canary)]
  D --> E[Canary Pods]
  C --> F[User Response]
  E --> F

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace data-transform cronjob: it should run hourly, read new files from a PVC-mounted /input, transform them with awk into /output, and post a summary to a central endpoint using a Bearer token from a Secret. Logs must go to a PVC at /logs. Use an InitContainer to install awk/coreutils; include readiness/liveness probes; enforce a Namespace NetworkPolicy that only allows egress to the central endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Design an hourly per-namespace CronJob. The Pod mounts /input, /output, and /logs PVCs, along with a Secret containing the Bearer token. Use an InitContainer to install awk and coreutils. The main container reads files from /input, transforms them using awk, writes results to /output, and posts a summary to a central endpoint. Include readiness and liveness probes for health monitoring. Apply a Namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy that restricts egress to only the central endpoint.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates CKAD-ready knowledge of CronJob patterns, PVCs, Secrets, InitContainers, health checks, and NetworkPolicy in a realistic data-processing workflow.

## Key Concepts

- **CronJob and Job lifecycle**: Understanding scheduling, concurrency policies, and job history limits
- **PVC mounts**: Using persistent volumes for input, output, and log storage within a namespace
- **Secrets**: Securely storing and mounting authentication tokens
- **InitContainers**: Pre-installing required tooling (awk, coreutils) before the main container runs
- **Readiness and Liveness probes**: Ensuring the pod is ready to serve traffic and remains healthy
- **NetworkPolicy**: Enforcing namespace-level egress restrictions to a central endpoint
- **Idempotent processing**: Handling file transformations safely across runs
- **Log management**: Persisting logs to PVC for debugging and auditing

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: data-transform
  namespace: data-processing
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: debian:book-slim
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apt-get update && apt-get install -y awk coreutils && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
          containers:
          - name: transformer
            image: alpine:latest
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |-
              apk add --no-cache curl awk
              for f in /input/*; do
                [ -f "$f" ] || continue
                awk '{print $0}' "$f" > "/output/$(basename $f)"
              done
              curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $(cat /secrets/token)" -d "@/logs/summary.json" https://central-endpoint.example.com/api/summary
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /ready
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /health
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
            volumeMounts:
            - name: input
              mountPath: /input
            - name: output
              mountPath: /output
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            - name: token-secret
              mountPath: /secrets
              readOnly: true
          volumes:
          - name: input
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: input-pvc
          - name: output
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: output-pvc
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          - name: token-secret
            secret:
              secretName: api-token-secret
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-egress
  namespace: data-processing
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: central-endpoint
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

## Trade-offs

- **InitContainer vs. custom image**: Using an InitContainer adds startup overhead but keeps the main image lightweight. A custom image with pre-installed tools eliminates this but increases maintenance.
- **ConcurrencyPolicy**: Using `Forbid` prevents overlapping jobs, simplifying idempotency but may cause missed runs if a job takes too long.
- **PVC vs. emptyDir**: PVCs provide persistence and data sharing across job runs but require proper storage provisioning. emptyDir is simpler but ephemeral.
- **NetworkPolicy granularity**: Restricting egress to specific pods enhances security but requires careful label management and may break if endpoints change.
- **Probe implementation**: HTTP probes require an endpoint; exec probes are more flexible but add complexity.

Include an InitContainer to install kubectl, a Secret for API credentials to a canary-control API, a TLS cert-rotation sidecar, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to internal endpoints. questions.question: In namespace ops, a Deployment 'orchestrator' with 2 replicas runs a sidecar that calls the Kubernetes API to manage worker Pods. Provide a minimal manifest patch to create a ServiceAccount 'orch-sa', a Role 'orch-pod-access' granting get/list/watch on Pods and get on ConfigMaps in the namespace, and a RoleBinding binding the SA to the Role; modify the Deployment to use 'orch-sa'; add resource requests/limits and readinessProbe on /healthz and livenessProbe on /readyz; include verification steps for RBAC and rollback?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests RBAC scoping, per-namespace access, and integration of security with deployment updates.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes RBAC: ServiceAccounts, Roles, RoleBindings
- Deployment serviceAccountName binding
- Health checks: readiness and liveness probes
- Resource requests/limits basics
- Rollback: patch reversal and resource cleanup

## Code Example
```yaml
# ServiceAccount
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: orch-sa
  namespace: ops
---
# Role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: orch-pod-access
  namespace: ops
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get"]
---
# RoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: orch-binding
  namespace: ops
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: orch-sa
  namespace: ops
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: orch-pod-access
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# Deployment patch (snippet)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: orchestrator
  namespace: ops
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: orch-sa
      containers:
      - name: orchestrator
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /readyz
            port: 80
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify RBAC in a multi-tenant scenario?  
- How would you roll back if a bug in RBAC grants unintended access?  
- What monitoring would you add to detect RBAC misconfigurations at runtime?

questions.question: In namespace bank, a Deployment named 'payments' with 2 replicas uses a feature-flag ConfigMap to toggle risk checks and write to a PVC. Provide a minimal manifest patch to: mount the ConfigMap at /etc/risk, annotate the Pod template with checksum/config to trigger rolling restarts when the ConfigMap changes, set resource requests/limits, add readinessProbe on /healthz and livenessProbe on /livez, add a preStop sleep of 5s, and create a PodDisruptionBudget requiring at least 1 healthy pod during maintenance. Additionally, add a Namespace ResourceQuota and LimitRange to cap resources. Explain verification under load and rollback strategies?
questions.answer: Patch the Payments Deployment to mount /etc/risk from a ConfigMap, annotate the Pod template with checksum/config to restart on changes, set cpu/mem requests/limits, add readinessProbe on /healthz and
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical Kubernetes patching: config-driven behavior, rolling restarts, probes, and safety nets via quota and PDB.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap-based feature flags with rolling restarts via checksum/config
- Probes (readiness and liveness) for service health
- Pod lifecycle: preStop grace period
- PodDisruptionBudget for maintenance safety
- Namespace quotas and limits to enforce resource usage

## Code Example
```yaml
# snippet illustrating patch targets
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments
  namespace: bank
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        checksum/config: <hash-of-configmap> # computed hash
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: payments
          image: payments:latest
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "256Mi"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /livez
              port: 8080
          lifecycle:
            preStop:
              exec:
                command: ["sleep", "5"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: risk-config
              mountPath: /etc/risk
      volumes:
        - name: risk-config
          configMap:
            name: risk-config
---
apiVersion: policy/v1beta1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: payments-pdb
  namespace: bank
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payments
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: bank-quota
  namespace: bank
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: bank-limits
  namespace: bank
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "512Mi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    type: Container
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test rolling restart triggered by ConfigMap changes in a live cluster?
- What changes would you make if you need zero-downtime deployments during quota exhaustion?

questions.question: Scenario: In a CKAD scenario, you must roll out a new image for a stateless API 'inventory-api' in namespace 'prod' with 2% traffic to the canary, using vanilla Kubernetes (no service mesh). Outline a practical canary strategy with two Deployments and two Services, explain how you split traffic without a mesh, and provide minimal manifests for the canary Deployment and a canary-facing Service, plus a rollback plan and how you'd validate under load?
questions.answer:
Two Deployments: stable and canary with distinct labels; two Services: inventory-svc (stable) and inventory-svc-canary (canary). Vanilla Kubernetes lacks built-in weighted routing, so use an Ingress controller with weighted backend configuration.

## Strategy
1. Deploy stable version (98% traffic)
2. Deploy canary version (2% traffic) 
3. Use Ingress annotations for weighted routing
4. Monitor and validate under load
5. Rollback by updating Ingress weights

## Core Manifests

```yaml
# Stable Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: inventory-api-stable
  namespace: prod
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: inventory-api
      version: stable
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: inventory-api
        version: stable
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: inventory-api
        image: repo/inventory-api:v1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

---
# Canary Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: inventory-api-canary
  namespace: prod
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: inventory-api
      version: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: inventory-api
        version: canary
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: inventory-api
        image: repo/inventory-api:canary
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

---
# Stable Service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: inventory-svc-stable
  namespace: prod
spec:
  selector:
    app: inventory-api
    version: stable
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080

---
# Canary Service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: inventory-svc-canary
  namespace: prod
spec:
  selector:
    app: inventory-api
    version: canary
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080

---
# Ingress with Weighted Routing
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: inventory-api-ingress
  namespace: prod
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: "HTTP"
    # NGINX Ingress weighted routing
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "2"
    # Alternative: Traefik weighted service
    # traefik.ingress.kubernetes.io/router.middlewares: "canary-weight@kubernetescrd"
spec:
  rules:
  - host: inventory-api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: inventory-svc-stable
            port:
              number: 80
```

## Rollback Plan
1. **Immediate rollback**: Update Ingress annotation `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "0"`
2. **Full rollback**: Delete canary Deployment and Service
3. **Rollback command**: `kubectl patch ingress inventory-api-ingress -n prod -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight":"0"}}}'`

## Validation Under Load
```bash
# Generate load with ApacheBench
ab -n 1000 -c 10 http://inventory-api.example.com/health

# Monitor traffic split
kubectl logs -n prod -l version=stable --tail=50
kubectl logs -n prod -l version=canary --tail=50

# Check metrics
kubectl top pods -n prod --l version=canary
kubectl get pods -n prod -l version=canary -w

# Validate canary receives ~2% of requests
for i in {1..100}; do curl -s http://inventory-api.example.com/health | grep -q "canary" && echo "Canary hit: $i"; done
```
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical canary workflows in vanilla Kubernetes, a common CKAD constraint requiring understanding of traffic splitting without service mesh.

## Key Concepts

- Canary rollout without service mesh using Ingress controllers
- Dual deployments with version-specific labels and selectors
- Ingress-based weighted routing annotations (NGINX/Traefik)
- Service isolation for stable and canary versions
- Rollback strategies using Ingress annotation updates
- Load validation using traffic generation and monitoring

## Implementation Details

The solution provides complete, production-ready manifests showing:
1. **Stable deployment** with higher replica count handling 98% traffic
2. **Canary deployment** with single replica for 2% traffic
3. **Separate services** enabling selective traffic routing
4. **Ingress configuration** with weighted routing using NGINX annotations
5. **Rollback commands** for immediate traffic re-routing
6. **Load validation** using ab, kubectl monitoring, and hit counting

This demonstrates practical CKAD-level knowledge of vanilla Kubernetes canary patterns, proper resource management, and operational validation techniques.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This checks secure defaults and practical CKAD patterns: ConfigMap/Secret usage, correct SecurityContext, and health checks.

## Key Concepts
- SecurityContext: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities: drop: ALL
- ConfigMap and Secret mounting into containers
- Resource requests/limits
- Liveness and readiness probes
- ClusterIP service exposure

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal Deployment skeleton (yaml)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: flask-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: flask-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: flask
        image: python:3.11-slim
        command: ["python", "/app/main.py"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: app-config
          mountPath: /app/config
        - name: api-key
          mountPath: /secrets
          readOnly: true
        securityContext:
          runAsUser: 1001
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          capabilities:
            drop: ["ALL"]
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 5000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 5000
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
      volumes:
      - name: app-config
        configMap:
          name: flask-config
      - name: api-key
        secret:
          secretName: flask-api-key
```

```yaml
# Minimal Service skeleton
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: flask-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: flask-app
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 5000
  type: ClusterIP
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you rotate the API_KEY secret without downtime?
- What are the trade-offs of using readOnlyRootFilesystem in production?

## Why This Is Asked

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
```

## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: namespace-scoped configs, Secrets, ConfigMaps, PVCs, InitContainers, Probes, and NetworkPolicies in a real-ops pattern.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped CronJob
- ConfigMap for policy definitions
- Secret for API token
- ConfigMap for status persistence
- PersistentVolumeClaim for logs
- InitContainer for tooling
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to policy portal

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal CronJob skeleton (placeholders:
# namespace: <target-namespace>, policy-config: <ConfigMap>, logs-pvc: <PVC>)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: image-policy-guardian
  namespace: <target-namespace>
spec:
  schedule: "0,30 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
            - name: fetch-tools
              image: appropriate/curl-jq
              command: ["sh","-c","echo init"]
          containers:
            - name: guardian
              image: <registry>/image-policy-guardian:latest
              volumeMounts:
                - name: logs
                  mountPath: /logs
          volumes:
            - name: logs
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: <logs-pvc>
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test failure modes (policy update, portal unreachable, token expiry)?
- What are the performance and RBAC trade-offs of namespace-scoped policies vs cluster-wide controllers?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assess practical security hardening in CKAD, plus safe rollouts and rollback.

- Container securityContext
- runAsNonRoot and runAsUser

```yaml

spec:

        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests YAML validation, policy enforcement, and per-namespace isolation using common CKAD primitives. It also probes integration hooks (ConfigMap, Secret, NetworkPolicy) and security concerns (TLS sidecar).

## Key Concepts
- yamllint/kubeval validation
- PodSpec resource requirements (requests/limits)
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- ConfigMap-driven batching (BATCH_SIZE)
- TLS sidecar and certificate rotation
- Namespace NetworkPolicy egress control

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton for the manifest guard Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: manifest-guard
  namespace: placeholder
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: manifest-guard
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: manifest-guard
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache yamllint curl jq kubeval"]
      containers:
      - name: guard
        image: busybox:latest
        # main loop logic (watch /manifests, lint, enforce) implemented in app
        command: ["/bin/sh"]
        args: ["-c", "run-guard.sh"]
      volumes:
      - name: manifests
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: manifests-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale this per namespace when manifests grow large?
- How would you adapt rules for different namespaces (e.g., exempting test namespaces)?

Logs to /logs on a PVC; InitContainer installs curl/jq; include readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the audit portal. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests CRD API access from a pod, ConfigMap-driven runtime configuration, Secret-based credentials, InitContainer usage, and proper observability with probes and PVC-backed logs. It also touches network isolation via NetworkPolicy in a CKAD-appropriate setting.

## Key Concepts

- CRD watch and API access from a namespace-scoped Pod
- ConfigMap as dynamic WINDOW configuration
- Secret-stored Bearer token for outbound POST
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- Readiness/Liveness probes for a long-running auditor
- Logs persisted to a PVC
- Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
data:
  audit-config: |
    window: 60
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
data:
  audit-token: <base64-encoded-token>
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: crd-auditor
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: crd-auditor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: crd-auditor
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: crd-auditor-sa
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /logs
          name: logs
      containers:
      - name: auditor
        image: my-registry/crd-auditor:latest
        env:
        - name: AUDIT_CONFIG
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: audit-config
              key: window
        - name: AUDIT_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: audit-token
              key: token
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /logs
          name: logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: crd-auditor-logs
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you simulate CRD changes locally for tests?
- How would you handle CRD API version upgrades and backward compatibility?

  E --> F[Write logs to /logs PVC]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace security auditor: a Deployment in each namespace runs a lightweight agent that lists RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and ServiceAccounts in that namespace, compares against a policy defined in a ConfigMap (rbac-policy), and posts a digest to a central audit endpoint using a Bearer token from a Secret. Logs go to a PVC at /logs; InitContainer installs curl/jq; include readiness/liveness probes; Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the audit endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Per-namespace Deployment runs a lightweight agent that lists RBAC objects (RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and ServiceAccounts) in that namespace, compares them against a policy defined in ConfigMap rbac-policy, and posts a digest to a central audit endpoint using a Bearer token from a Secret. Logs are written to a PVC at /logs. An InitContainer installs curl/jq. The Deployment includes readiness and liveness probes. A Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the audit endpoint only.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests practical RBAC auditing skills within the CKAD scope, including multi-namespace governance, secure logging, and egress constraints.

## Key Concepts

- **RBAC objects**: RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and ServiceAccounts at the namespace level
- **ConfigMap-driven policy**: External policy reference for audit rules
- **Namespace-scoped auditing**: Decentralized audit model vs. cluster-wide approaches
- **Pod lifecycle**: InitContainers for tool installation, readiness/liveness probes for reliability
- **Storage**: PVC-backed logging at /logs
- **Network security**: Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the audit endpoint

## Trade-offs

- **Pros**: Decentralized scaling, namespace isolation, ConfigMap flexibility for policy updates
- **Cons**: Resource overhead per namespace, potential for policy drift, Secret management complexity, duplicated effort across namespaces

## Minimal YAML Skeleton

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: rbac-policy
data:
  policy.yaml: |-
    allowedBindings:
      - kind: User
        name: admin
    maxServiceAccounts: 10
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: audit-credentials
type: Opaque
stringData:
  bearer-token: <token>
  endpoint: https://audit.example.com/api/v1/digest
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: audit-logs
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: rbac-auditor
  namespace: target-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: rbac-auditor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: rbac-auditor
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: install-tools
          image: busybox
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - "wget -qO /tools/curl https://curl.se/download && wget -qO /tools/jq https://github.com/jqlang/jq/releases/download/jq-1.7.1/jq-linux64 && chmod +x /tools/*"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
      containers:
        - name: auditor
          image: alpine:latest
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              apk add --no-cache curl jq
              while true; do
                kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings,serviceaccounts -o json > /logs/snapshot.json
                curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d @/logs/snapshot.json $ENDPOINT
                sleep 3600
              done
          env:
            - name: TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: audit-credentials
                  key: bearer-token
            - name: ENDPOINT
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: audit-credentials
                  key: endpoint
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
        - name: logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: audit-logs
        - name: tools
          emptyDir: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: audit-egress-restrict
  namespace: target-ns
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: rbac-auditor
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: audit-api
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443
```
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Namespace] --> B[RBAC Auditor Deployment]
  B --> C[List RBAC objects]
  C --> D[Compare to policy ConfigMap]
  D --> E[Digest & POST to audit endpoint]
  E --> F[Write logs to /logs PVC]
  F --> G[NetworkPolicy restricts egress]

questions.question: In namespace nightly, a CronJob named 'db-backup' schedules daily backups of a PV-backed database and writes into /backups. Provide a minimal manifest patch to: enforce single concurrent job, trim history for success/failure, set startingDeadlineSeconds, bootstrap a backup script via an InitContainer from a ConfigMap named 'backup-config' into /backup, ensure non-root, and add basic readiness/liveness probes. Include how you'd test load and rollback?
questions.answer: Patch CronJob to: concurrencyPolicy: Forbid, startingDeadlineSeconds: 3600, successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3, failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1; add an InitContainer 'init-backup' that copies /config/backup.sh i
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to safely patch CronJobs, leverage InitContainers for bootstrapping from ConfigMaps, enforce security context non-root, and apply sensible history limits. It also probes operational testing and rollback strategy for scheduled jobs.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob controls: concurrencyPolicy, history limits, startingDeadlineSeconds
- InitContainers and ConfigMaps for bootstrapping
- PodSecurityContext: runAsNonRoot
- Probes on Jobs' pods and resource requests/limits
- Testing and rollback of scheduled jobs

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal CronJob patch outline (conceptual)
apiVersion: batch/v1beta1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-backup
  namespace: nightly
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 3600
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: backup-sa
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
          initContainers:
            - name: init-backup
              image: busybox
              command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /config/backup.sh /backup/backup.sh"]
              volumeMounts:
                - name: backup-config
                  mountPath: /config
                - name: backups
                  mountPath: /backup
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: backup-tool:latest
              resources:
                requests:
                  memory: "128Mi"
                  cpu: "100m"
                limits:
                  memory: "256Mi"
                  cpu: "500m"
              readinessProbe:
                httpGet:
                  path: /livez
                  port: 8080
              livenessProbe:
                httpGet:
                  path: /readyz
                  port: 8080
              volumeMounts:
                - name: backups
                  mountPath: /backups
          volumes:
            - name: backups
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: backups-pvc
            - name: backup-config
              configMap:
                name: backup-config
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you monitor backup duration and alert on failures?
- How would you handle a failed run without losing data?
