questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests your ability to design an isolated audit pattern with in-cluster API access, RBAC scoping, and security boundaries.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped RBAC for fine-grained access control
- In-cluster API access using Secrets and ConfigMaps
- PVC-backed persistent logs and InitContainers for tooling
- NetworkPolicy for egress control to restrict traffic to the collector
- Health checks (readiness/liveness probes) for reliability

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: audit-baseline
  namespace: example
data:
  baseline.json: |
    {"pods": [], "services": []}
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: audit-auth
  namespace: example
type: Opaque
stringData:
  token: " collector-auth-token"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: audit-logs
  namespace: example
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: audit-agent
  namespace: example
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: audit-agent
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: audit-agent
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl jq && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tools
          mountPath: /tools
      containers:
      - name: auditor
        image: your-audit-image:latest
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "while true; do audit.sh; sleep 60; done"]
        env:
        - name: COLLECTOR_URL
          value: "http://collector.audit-system.svc.cluster.local:8080"
        - name: AUTH_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: audit-auth
              key: token
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        - name: tools
          mountPath: /tools
        - name: baseline
          mountPath: /baseline
          readOnly: true
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: audit-logs
      - name: tools
        emptyDir: {}
      - name: baseline
        configMap:
          name: audit-baseline
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: audit-egress-restrict
  namespace: example
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: audit-agent
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: audit-system
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: collector
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
```

## Trade-offs
- **Pros**: Namespace isolation ensures audit agents don't interfere; NetworkPolicy provides security; PVC enables log persistence for compliance
- **Cons**: Each namespace requires separate Deployment (use Operator/Controller to automate); InitContainer adds startup latency; per-namespace RBAC management overhead increases with cluster size

flowchart TD
  N[Namespace] --> A[Audit-Agent Deployment]
  A --> B[Baseline ConfigMap]
  A --> C[Central Collector Service]
  A --> D[Logs PVC /logs]

Write tagged manifests to /tagged, and POST a summary to http://tagger-portal:8080/api/v1/summary when validation passes. Include an InitContainer to install kubeval/yamllint, a Secret for API creds, a TLS sidecar for cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: PVC handling, InitContainers, ConfigMaps/Secrets, simple templating, manifest validation, and network policies. It avoids previous questions by focusing on label injection and re-validation flow.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainer tool installation (kubeval, yamllint)
- ConfigMap/Secret usage for config and credentials
- PVC-mounted manifest discovery and simple file loop
- Lightweight templating to inject labels
- Validation with kubeval/yamllint and conditional POST
- TLS sidecar and readiness/liveness probes
- Namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy restricting egress

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: manifest-tagger
  namespace: <target-namespace>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: manifest-tagger
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache kubeval yamllint curl jq && echo done"]
      containers:
      - name: tagger
        image: your-registry/manifest-tagger:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /manifests
        - name: tagged
          mountPath: /tagged
        - name: config
          mountPath: /config
        env:
        - name: TAG_KEY
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: tagger-config
              key: tag-key
        - name: API_BASE
          value: http://tagger-portal:8080/api/v1/summary
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      - name: tls-sidecar
        image: curlimages/curl:7.88.1
        # TLS rotation logic assumed
      volumes:
      - name: manifests
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: manifests-pvc
      - name: tagged
        emptyDir: {}
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: tagger-config
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the end-to-end flow locally? 
- How would you handle a manifest that fails validation after tagging? 
- How would you scale if the namespace has hundreds of manifests per minute?

## Why This Is Asked

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1beta1

metadata:

          serviceAccountName: config-validator-sa

          volumes:
            - name: manifests
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: manifests-pvc
```

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace Resource Guard: a CronJob that runs every 5 minutes to audit Deployments for missing resource requests/limits and patch them with defaults from a ConfigMap named resource-defaults. Persist last audit status in a namespace quota-status ConfigMap and write logs to a PVC at /logs. Use an InitContainer to install kubectl and jq; add readiness/liveness probes; and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the API server. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Audit every namespace for missing resource requests/limits in Deployments and patch to defaults from a ConfigMap, using a CronJob per-namespace. Logs to PVC at /logs, status in quota-status ConfigMap,
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design a namespace-scoped automation that enforces resource policies, uses ConfigMaps for defaults, and reports via PVCs and status ConfigMaps. Requires practical use of CronJobs, InitContainers, and patching, plus RBAC and network controls.

## Key Concepts

- Namespace-scoped CronJob and per-namespace scope
- Deployments, resourceRequests, resourceLimits
- ConfigMap as defaults and heartbeat/status store
- InitContainer for kubectl/jq installation
- Patch operations to enforce defaults (idempotent)
- Logs via PVC and readiness/liveness probes
- Namespace NetworkPolicy limiting egress to API server

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: ns-resource-guard
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: ns-resource-guard-sa
          initContainers:
          - name: install-tools
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq && echo done"]
          containers:
          - name: guard
            image: bitnami/kubectl:1.28
            command: ["sh", "-c", "kubectl get deploy --all-namespaces -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.template.spec.containers[]?.resources?.requests==null or .spec.template.spec.containers[]?.resources?.limits==null) | .metadata.namespace' | sort -u | while read ns; do kubectl -n $ns patch deploy --type=json -p '[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources","value": {"requests": {"cpu":"100m","memory":"128Mi"}, "limits": {"cpu":"200m","memory":"256Mi"}}}]' || true; done"]
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          - name: defaults
            configMap:
              name: resource-defaults
          tolerations:
          - operator: Exists
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you ensure patch idempotence and handle multi-container deployments?
- What RBAC would you restrict for the CronJob's service account?

  P --> S[Update quota-status ConfigMap with last result]
  JC --> L[Logs to PVC at /logs]
  L --> API[Log visibility & auditing]
  API --> N

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a new, per-namespace data-collector that subscribes to an in-cluster message bus (NATS) and writes results to a time-series store, ensuring at-least-once delivery with a DLQ. Include an InitContainer to install runtime deps, a ConfigMap for BATCH_SIZE, a Secret for creds, a Sidecar for TLS cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, and a NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the TSDB, a DLQ path on a PVC, and a minimal YAML skeleton. Explain trade-offs?
questions.answer: Design a per-namespace data-collector that subscribes to NATS, batches events, and writes to InfluxDB with at-least-once delivery and a DLQ. Use an InitContainer to install runtime dependencies, a ConfigMap for BATCH_SIZE configuration, and a Secret for credentials. Include a sidecar for TLS certificate rotation, implement readiness and liveness probes, and restrict egress traffic to the time-series database using a NetworkPolicy. Mount a PVC for the dead-letter queue path and provide a minimal YAML skeleton for deployment.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests comprehensive CKAD skills including per-namespace isolation, configuration management with ConfigMaps and Secrets, InitContainers for dependency setup, sidecar patterns for auxiliary services, health probes for reliability, RBAC for security, and NetworkPolicy for traffic control.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainers for runtime dependency installation
- ConfigMaps and Secrets for configuration and credential management
- Sidecar containers for TLS certificate rotation
- Readiness and liveness probes for health monitoring
- Namespace isolation and NetworkPolicy for security boundaries
- Dead-letter queue implementation on PVC storage
- At-least-once delivery semantics with batch processing

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ns-collector
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: install-deps
    image: alpine:latest
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'apk add --no-cache nats-cli']
  containers:
  - name: collector
    image: collector:latest
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: ns-collector-config
    - secretRef:
        name: ns-collector-creds
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 8080
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 8080
    volumeMounts:
    - name: dlq-pvc
      mountPath: /dlq
  - name: tls-sidecar
    image: tls-sidecar:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tls-certs
      mountPath: /etc/tls
  volumes:
  - name: dlq-pvc
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: dlq-pvc
  - name: tls-certs
    secret:
      secretName: tls-certs
```

## Trade-offs
- **At-least-once vs exactly-once**: At-least-once provides simpler implementation but may cause duplicate processing
- **Sidecar overhead**: TLS rotation adds resource consumption but improves security
- **PVC vs temporary storage**: PVC provides persistence but increases complexity and cost
- **NetworkPolicy restrictions**: Improve security but may impact debugging and monitoring capabilities

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests the ability to operate within a namespace context while working with core Kubernetes primitives: ConfigMaps for configuration, Secrets for sensitive data, and a controller-like process to automate cleanup tasks. It also assesses knowledge of observability (logging to PVC), container tooling (InitContainers), health checks, and network security.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped RBAC for job listing and deletion
- ConfigMap-driven TTL configuration
- Secrets for API key storage
- Kubernetes API access patterns (list, watch, delete Jobs)
- PVC-backed logging
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- Readiness and liveness probes
- NetworkPolicy for egress restrictions

## Trade-offs
- **Per-namespace vs. cluster-wide**: A per-namespace deployment provides isolation and TTL flexibility per namespace but increases resource overhead. A cluster-wide operator is more efficient but less flexible.
- **Polling vs. watching**: Polling is simpler to implement but introduces latency; watching is more efficient but adds complexity.
- **Push vs. pull for digests**: Sending digests immediately provides real-time visibility but increases API calls; batching reduces load but delays information.

- What happens if the external portal is unreachable?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace TLS certificate rotation runner: a long-running Deployment in each namespace polls a CA API every 15 minutes for renewed certificates. If the certificate changes, update the tls-cert Secret in that namespace and trigger a rolling restart of all Deployments by annotating them with a rotation timestamp. apiVersion: apps/v1
```yaml
kind: Deployment
metadata:

spec:
  replicas: 1

      initContainers:

      containers:

      volumes:
        - name: logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: tls-rotator-logs
```

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design a one-off reconciliation Job that interacts with external services, ensures idempotent processing via a fingerprint, and properly configures InitContainers, Secrets, and backoff behavior. It also checks handling of PVC-mounted data and rollback planning.

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-ingest-audit-reconcile
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 900
  template:
    metadata:
      name: data-ingest-audit-reconcile
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: reconciler
        image: your-registry/audit-client:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "set -e; /bin/reconcile.sh /data/manifests.yaml"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /data
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq && curl -L -o /usr/local/bin/yq https://github.com/mikefarah/yq/releases/download/v4.9.6/yq_linux_amd64 && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/yq"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /data
      restartPolicy: Never
      volumes:
      - name: manifests
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: data-manifests
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: regcred
```


## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test idempotency in CI/CD?
- How would you scale this for larger manifest sets without timeouts?
- How would you monitor and rollback on failure?

## Why This Is Asked
Tests knowledge of VolumeSnapshot APIs, per-namespace scheduling, and how to surface status and logs from a snapshot operation while enforcing network boundaries.

## Code Example
```yaml
# CronJob skeleton
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: snapshot-app-data
  namespace: <ns>
spec:
  schedule: "0,30 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: init-tools
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl kubectl; sleep 0"]
          containers:
          - name: snapshotter
            image: my-snapshot-agent:latest
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          volumes:

              claimName: logs-<ns>
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          nodeSelector:
            kubernetes.io/os: linux
```

```yaml

metadata:

```

questions.question: Design a per-namespace canary promoter: a Deployment in namespace N watches a ConfigMap canary-config (fields: canaryPercent, canaryImage, prodImage, canaryWindowSeconds). If canaryPercent > 0, it updates the target Deployment's image to canaryImage; after canaryWindowSeconds, promote to prodImage. Logs go to a PVC at /logs. InitContainer installs curl/jq; include readiness/liveness probes; Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the image registry. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
A per-namespace canary promoter is implemented as a Deployment in namespace N that watches a ConfigMap named `canary-config`. This ConfigMap contains four key fields: `canaryPercent`, `canaryImage`, `prodImage`, and `canaryWindowSeconds`.

When `canaryPercent > 0`, the promoter updates the target Deployment's image to `canaryImage` using an idempotent kubectl patch. After `canaryWindowSeconds` elapses, it promotes the image to `prodImage`. All logs are written to a PVC mounted at `/logs`.

The solution includes an InitContainer that installs curl and jq for API interactions, along with readiness and liveness probes for health monitoring. A Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to only the image registry.

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: canary-config
  namespace: N
data:
  canaryPercent: "0"
  canaryImage: ""
  prodImage: ""
  canaryWindowSeconds: "300"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: canary-promoter
  namespace: N
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: alpine
        command: [sh, -c, "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: promoter
        image: canary-promoter:latest
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: canary-logs
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: registry-only
  namespace: N
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: registry
```

**Trade-offs:** The ConfigMap-driven approach offers simplicity but introduces a control loop that must handle reconciliation edge cases. PVC logging provides persistence but requires storage provisioning. NetworkPolicy egress restrictions enhance security but add operational complexity.

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace static-site-serve: a Deployment running nginx with 2 replicas, mounting a PVC at /usr/share/nginx/html for static files, a ConfigMap site-content for index.html/assets, and a Secret for basic auth credentials; also mount a nginx.conf from another ConfigMap to enable basic auth. Expose via a ClusterIP Service. Add readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace-wide ResourceQuota and LimitRange to cap CPU/memory. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: I would implement a Deployment with 2 nginx replicas, mounting the PVC at /usr/share/nginx/html and using a ConfigMap site-content for the static files, plus a Secret for credentials and a ConfigMap f
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Static-site serving in a per-namespace context tests PVC/ConfigMap/Secret integration, nginx config via ConfigMap, and safe multi-tenant limits with quotas and limits.

## Key Concepts

- Deployments, replicas, and rolling updates
- PVCs for shared data, ConfigMaps for content and nginx.conf, Secrets for credentials
- nginx basic auth integration via config map
- ClusterIP Service exposure and Readiness/Liveness probes
- Namespace ResourceQuota and LimitRange for multi-tenant safety

## Code Example

```yaml
# Minimal skeleton showing core resources
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: site-content
data:
  index.html: |-
    <html>...</html>
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: nginx-config
data:
  nginx.conf: |
    server { listen 80; location / { auth_basic "Restricted"; auth_basic_user_file /etc/nginx/.htpasswd; root /usr/share/nginx/html; } }
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: basic-auth
type: Opaque
data:
  # base64(encoded username:password)
  htpasswd: <base64>
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: static-site
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:stable
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        volumeMounts:
        - name: site
          mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
        - name: config
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
          subPath: nginx.conf
        - name: auth
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/.htpasswd
          subPath: htpasswd
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: site
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: static-site-pvc
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: nginx-config
      - name: auth
        secret:
          secretName: basic-auth
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: static-site-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: static-site
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
    protocol: TCP
  type: ClusterIP
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: static-site-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you update content without downtime?
- How would you rotate credentials and reflect it in nginx without redeploying pods?

  B --> E[Secret: basic-auth]
  B --> F[Service: static-site-svc (ClusterIP)]
  A --> G[ResourceQuota]
  A --> H[LimitRange]

Add a readinessProbe on /health and livenessProbe on /ready, resource requests, a 10s preStop, and a PodDisruptionBudget requiring at least 1 healthy pod. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to coordinate init-time setup with a running app, ensuring idempotent migrations and safe rollbacks in a realistic CKAD scenario.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainer orchestration and order
- PVC-mounted data initialization
- Probes for readiness/liveness under patch
- PodDisruptionBudget and graceful shutdown
- Rollback strategies in Kubernetes manifests

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal patch skeleton (illustrative)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: migrate
        image: migrate-image:latest
        command: ["/usr/local/bin/migrate"]
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /data
          name: data
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: webapp:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /data
          name: data
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
      volumes:
      - name: data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: data-pvc
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: webapp-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you ensure the InitContainer migration runs idempotently across restarts?
- What metrics or events would you monitor to validate rollout safety and rollback effectiveness?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace database-migration pipeline: a CronJob in each namespace triggers a Job that runs a SQL schema migration against the tenant database service in that namespace, sourcing the script from a ConfigMap and credentials from a Secret, using an InitContainer to install a SQL client, mounting a PVC at /logs to store the run logs, with readiness/liveness probes on the Pod, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy that restricts egress to the tenant DB endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
## CKAD: Per-Namespace Database Migration Pipeline

### Complete YAML Skeleton

```yaml
---
# 1. ConfigMap: SQL Migration Script
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: migration-script
  namespace: tenant-{{ .Values.tenantId }}
data:
  migrate.sql: |
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
      id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
      email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
      created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
    );
    ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS phone VARCHAR(20);
---
# 2. Secret: Database Credentials
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: tenant-{{ .Values.tenantId }}
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DB_HOST: "tenant-db"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_NAME: "tenant_db"
  DB_USER: "{{ .Values.dbUser }}"
  DB_PASSWORD: "{{ .Values.dbPassword }}"
---
# 3. PVC: Persistent Volume for Logs
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: migration-logs
  namespace: tenant-{{ .Values.tenantId }}
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: standard
---
# 4. CronJob: Scheduler
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-migration-cron
  namespace: tenant-{{ .Values.tenantId }}
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # 2 AM daily
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 2
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          initContainers:
            - name: install-sql-client
              image: alpine:3.18
              command:
                - sh
                - -c
                - |
                  apk add --no-cache postgresql-client jq
                  echo "SQL client installed"
              volumeMounts:
                - name: shared-logs
                  mountPath: /logs
          containers:
            - name: migration
              image: alpine:3.18
              command:
                - sh
                - -c
                - |
                  export $(cat /secrets/db.env | xargs) && \
                  psql -h $DB_HOST -p $DB_PORT -U $DB_USER -d $DB_NAME -f /scripts/migrate.sql \
                  2>&1 | tee /logs/migration-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log
              env:
                - name: MIGRATION_ID
                  valueFrom:
                    fieldRef:
                      fieldPath: metadata.labels['controller-uid']
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: db-credentials
              volumeMounts:
                - name: scripts
                  mountPath: /scripts
                  readOnly: true
                - name: shared-logs
                  mountPath: /logs
              readinessProbe:
                exec:
                  command:
                    - sh
                    - -c
                    - "[ -f /logs/migration-complete ]"
                initialDelaySeconds: 5
                periodSeconds: 10
              livenessProbe:
                exec:
                  command:
                    - sh
                    - -c
                    - "ps aux | grep -v grep | grep psql"
                initialDelaySeconds: 15
                periodSeconds: 30
              resources:
                requests:
                  cpu: 100m
                  memory: 128Mi
                limits:
                  cpu: 500m
                  memory: 512Mi
          volumes:
            - name: scripts
              configMap:
                name: migration-script
            - name: shared-logs
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: migration-logs
---
# 5. NetworkPolicy: Restrict Egress to Tenant DB
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-migration-netpol
  namespace: tenant-{{ .Values.tenantId }}
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: db-migration
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector: {}
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: tenant-db
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
```

### Trade-offs Discussion

**ConfigMap Update Propagation**
- ConfigMaps mounted as volumes are synced within ~1 minute via kubelet periodic sync
- For critical migrations, consider using `subPath` (but this prevents live updates) or rebuilding with new ConfigMap hash label
- Alternative: bake scripts into image for guaranteed atomicity, at the cost of flexibility

**Secret Rotation Challenges**
- Secrets updated while Job is running won't be reflected (mounted secrets are static at pod start)
- Long-running migrations may need in-app re-authentication capability
- Secret TTL/expiration requires pod restart to pick up new credentials
- Consider external secrets operators (ESO, ASM) for dynamic secret injection

**PVC Lifecycle Management**
- PVC must exist before Job runs; need proper ordering via Helm hooks or ArgoCD sync waves
- Log retention policy needs separate cleanup mechanism (cronjob, TTL, or external storage)
- `ReadWriteOnce` limits concurrent Job access; consider `ReadWriteMany` with NFS or EFS for multi-tenant
- Storage costs scale with namespace count

**Probe Configuration**
- Liveness probe on migration container can be problematic: if migration takes >30s, probe may kill pod mid-execution
- Readiness probe checks "am I ready to serve requests?" — not meaningful for one-shot Jobs
- For Jobs, consider removing probes entirely or using exec probe with short failure threshold

**NetworkPolicy Limitations**
- Most CNIs don't enforce egress by default; may require CNI-specific plugins (Calico, Cilium)
- DNS resolution (kube-dns) must be explicitly allowed if credentials are fetched from external sources
- Test NetworkPolicy in staging; misconfiguration blocks ALL pod communication

**CronJob → Job Trigger Pattern**
- `concurrencyPolicy: Forbid` prevents overlapping migrations (good for data integrity)
- Alternative `Replace` kills previous Job before starting new one (risky for long migrations)
- Job history limits balance debugging needs vs. namespace object count

**InitContainer Trade-offs**
- Runs every Job execution; adds ~5-10s latency per migration
- Multi-stage image builds can eliminate InitContainer (pre-install psql in main image)
- Security: smaller InitContainer image (Alpine) reduces attack surface vs. full psql image
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Matters for CKAD

This question validates mastery of **production-grade Kubernetes patterns** — not just textbook syntax. It combines seven CKAD concepts (CronJob, Job, InitContainer, ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, NetworkPolicy) in a realistic multi-tenant operational scenario.

## Key Concepts Tested

| Concept | Role in Pipeline |
|---------|-----------------|
| **CronJob** | Schedules periodic migrations with concurrency control |
| **Job** | Executes single migration attempt (created by CronJob) |
| **InitContainer** | Prepares SQL client before main container runs |
| **ConfigMap** | Versioned, injectable migration scripts without image rebuilds |
| **Secret** | Secure credential injection with namespace isolation |
| **PVC** | Persistent log storage for debugging and audit trail |
| **NetworkPolicy** | Enforces zero-trust network per namespace |

## Critical Design Decisions

1. **Why InitContainer for SQL client?**
   - Keeps main image minimal (security) and migration image separate from app image
   - Trade-off: adds startup latency and complexity

2. **Why PVC at /logs?**
   - Jobs are ephemeral; logs would be lost without persistence
   - Enables audit trail and post-mortem debugging

3. **Why NetworkPolicy egress restriction?**
   - Tenant database should only receive connections from migration Jobs
   - Prevents lateral movement in case of pod compromise

4. **Why probes on Job Pod?**
   - Liveness: ensures stuck jobs are restarted (but can cause issues with long migrations)
   - Readiness: typically irrelevant for Jobs, but included per question requirements

## Common Pitfalls Candidates Miss

- Forgetting `restartPolicy: OnFailure` (Jobs require explicit restart policy)
- Not handling ConfigMap subPath mounting (prevents live updates)
- Missing PVC lifecycle management (orphan storage, cost accumulation)
- NetworkPolicy blocking DNS (common exam/interview mistake)
- Not considering concurrent Job execution (data corruption risk)

## Real-World Extensions

- **Argo Workflows**: For complex multi-step migrations with retry logic
- **Flyway/ Liquibase**: Tool-based migrations with built-in versioning
- **External Secrets Operator**: Dynamic credential rotation without pod restarts
- **Cilium NetworkPolicy**: More expressive than standard K8s NetworkPolicy

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace config-reloader: a Deployment that mounts a ConfigMap as /config, watches for changes using a lightweight inotify-based watcher to trigger a SIGHUP reload of the main process, and POSTs a change-summary to http://config-portal:7000/api/v1/reload; include InitContainer to install inotify-tools, a Secret for API creds, a TLS-sidecar for cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
## Per-Namespace Config-Reloader Design

### YAML Skeleton

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: config-reloader-ns
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-creds
  namespace: config-reloader-ns
type: Opaque
stringData:
  API_KEY: "<key>"
  API_SECRET: "<secret>"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: config-reloader-ns
data:
  config.yaml: |
    key: value
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: config-reloader
  namespace: config-reloader-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: config-reloader
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: config-reloader
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: inotify-installer
          image: busybox
          command: ['sh', '-c', 'cp /tools/inotifywait /usr/local/bin/ && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/inotifywait']
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: your-app:latest
          env:
            - name: API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: api-creds
                  key: API_KEY
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
              readOnly: true
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
          lifecycle:
            preStop:
              exec:
                command: ['sh', '-c', 'kill -HUP $(cat /var/run/app.pid)']
        - name: watcher
          image: your-watcher:latest
          env:
            - name: API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: api-creds
                  key: API_KEY
            - name: TARGET_PID
              value: '1'
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
              readOnly: true
          command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              inotifywait -m -e modify,move,create,delete /config |
              while read path action file; do
                echo "Config changed: $file"
                kill -HUP $TARGET_PID
                curl -s -X POST http://config-portal:7000/api/v1/reload \
                  -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
                  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
                  -d '{"namespace":"$NAMESPACE","file":"$file","action":"$action"}'
              done
        - name: tls-sidecar
          image: cert-rotation-sidecar:latest
          securityContext:
            capabilities:
              add: ['NET_BIND_SERVICE']
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "16Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "50m"
              memory: "32Mi"
      volumes:
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: app-config
        - name: tools
          emptyDir: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: egress-restrict
  namespace: config-reloader-ns
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: config-portal
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 7000
```

### Trade-offs

| Aspect | Consideration |
|--------|----------------|
| **inotify vs. polling** | inotify is event-driven and efficient but requires inotify-tools in the InitContainer, increasing image complexity. Polling is simpler but wastes resources. |
| **Sidecar vs. injected init** | The sidecar pattern is portable and shares the pod but adds resource overhead. Sidecar injection via mutating webhook is cleaner but requires additional cluster setup. |
| **SIGHUP reload** | Reliable for many daemons (nginx, Apache) but not universal. Applications must explicitly handle SIGHUP; otherwise, the signal terminates them. |
| **TLS sidecar** | Handles certificate rotation without modifying the application but adds complexity and potential race conditions during rotation. Consider a service mesh such as Istio for managed TLS. |
| **NetworkPolicy scope** | Namespace-level restriction is a security best practice but requires a cluster-wide NetworkPolicy plugin (Calico, Cilium). Verify that your CNI supports it on managed Kubernetes. |
| **API credentials in Secret** | Secrets are base64-encoded by default, not encrypted. Enable EncryptionConfiguration at rest and use RBAC to restrict access. |

### Implementation Notes

1. **InitContainer workaround**: Since inotify-tools is not always available in application images, copy it from a helper image or use a dedicated watcher image (e.g., `vimag/inotify`).
2. **PID handling**: If the main application does not write its PID to file, rely on a liveness probe to verify health after SIGHUP rather than depending on a PID file.
3. **Deduplication**: Rapid config changes can flood the API. Consider debouncing with a small buffer window.
4. **Graceful shutdown**: The preStop hook sends SIGHUP before termination, preventing dropped requests.

### Alternative Approaches

- **Kubernetes watches**: Use `list/watch` on ConfigMaps via the API with `reflector` or `client-go` informer for a more Kubernetes-native approach.
- **Operators**: For complex scenarios, consider a controller that reconciles config changes and manages rollouts automatically (e.g., the Reloader project).

2. **Container orchestration**: InitContainers for setup tasks, sidecar pattern for auxiliary containers
3. **Security**: Secrets for sensitive data, NetworkPolicies for network isolation
4. **Observability**: Readiness and liveness probes for reliability and uptime
5. **Process signaling**: SIGHUP reload semantics and lifecycle hooks

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD ability to compose a small, namespace-scoped verification workflow that touches ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, and network policy.

## Key Concepts
- Deployment with InitContainer
- ConfigMap-based allowlist
- Secret-based credentials and TLS sidecar
- PVC-mounted /secrets and lightweight parsing with jq/yq
- Readiness/Liveness probes and per-namespace NetworkPolicy

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: compliance-api-creds
  namespace: default
data:
  token: <base64>
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: secret-allowlist
  namespace: default
data:
  allowed_keys: |
    tls.key
    tls.crt
    creds
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: secret-compliance-checker
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: secret-compliance-checker
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: install-tools
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache jq curl && wget -O - https://github.com/yq-dev/yq/releases/latest/download/yq_linux_amd64 -O /usr/local/bin/yq && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/yq"]
      containers:
        - name: checker
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "./check.sh"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: secrets
              mountPath: /secrets
      volumes:
        - name: secrets
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: secret-storage
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 8080
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 10
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale and test this in a real cluster?
- What are risks with mounting Secrets in /secrets and how to mitigate?

questions.question: In namespace analytics, create a CronJob named 'db-backfill' that runs daily at 02:00 to execute a Postgres backfill against db.analytics.svc.cluster.local:5432. Mount /scripts from ConfigMap 'backfill-scripts' and load credentials from Secret 'db-creds' as envs. Use image postgres:15-alpine; run psql to /scripts/backfill.sql. Set concurrencyPolicy: Forbid, backoffLimit: 3, successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and explain verification and rollback steps?
questions.answer: Create a CronJob named 'db-backfill' in the 'analytics' namespace that runs daily at 02:00 UTC. Use the image 'postgres:15-alpine' and execute 'psql -f /scripts/backfill.sql' against the database at 'db.analytics.svc.cluster.local:5432'. Mount the /scripts directory from ConfigMap 'backfill-scripts' and inject database credentials from Secret 'db-creds' as environment variables. Configure the job with concurrencyPolicy set to 'Forbid', backoffLimit set to 3, and successfulJobsHistoryLimit set to 3.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question assesses practical CKAD-level skills in CronJobs, Secrets, ConfigMaps, and job lifecycle management, including verification and rollback strategies.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob and Job resources using the batch/v1 API
- ConfigMap volume mounts for configuration and script files
- Secret injection as environment variables
- CronJob policies: concurrencyPolicy, backoffLimit, and successfulJobsHistoryLimit

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-backfill
  namespace: analytics
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  backoffLimit: 3
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: db-backfill
            image: postgres:15-alpine
            env:
            - name: PGHOST
              value: db.analytics.svc.cluster.local
            - name: PGPORT
              value: "5432"
            - name: PGUSER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-creds
                  key: username
            - name: PGPASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-creds
                  key: password
            command:
            - psql
            - -f
            - /scripts/backfill.sql
            volumeMounts:
            - name: scripts
              mountPath: /scripts
          volumes:
          - name: scripts
            configMap:
              name: backfill-scripts
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Verification Steps
1. Apply the manifest: `kubectl apply -f db-backfill-cronjob.yaml`
2. Verify creation: `kubectl get cronjob db-backfill -n analytics`
3. Check resource details: `kubectl describe cronjob db-backfill -n analytics`
4. View scheduled jobs: `kubectl get jobs -n analytics --watch`
5. Inspect pod logs: `kubectl logs <pod-name> -n analytics`

## Rollback Steps
1. Immediate: `kubectl delete cronjob db-backfill -n analytics`
2. Restore previous version: `kubectl apply -f db-backfill-cronjob.yaml.backup`
3. Suspend job temporarily: `kubectl patch cronjob db-backfill -n analytics -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'`

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace canary-automation pattern: a CronJob runs every 5 minutes to promote a canary release based on a ConfigMap named canary-config that contains targetImage and canaryPercent for the Deployment in the same namespace. A separate canary-status ConfigMap records last rollout status; patch the Deployment to roll out the new image using RollingUpdate (maxSurge:1, maxUnavailable:0) and traffic shifting for canary pods. Logs go to a PVC mounted at /logs. InitContainer installs jq; add readiness/liveness probes; Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the private registry. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: This CronJob-driven pattern automates canary deployments within a single namespace using standard Kubernetes primitives. Every 5 minutes, the job reconciles the canary deployment by reading targetImage and canaryPercent from a canary-config ConfigMap. A separate canary-status ConfigMap persists rollout history for auditability. The Deployment uses RollingUpdate (maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0) for controlled updates, while traffic shifting routes requests to canary pods. An InitContainer pre-installs jq for JSON processing. Pod health is validated via readiness and liveness probes. Logs are persisted to a PVC mounted at /logs. A Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the private registry. The following YAML skeleton implements this pattern, along with key trade-offs.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question assesses the ability to design a namespace-scoped canary workflow using standard CKAD primitives: CronJob, ConfigMaps, and Deployment rolling updates. It also tests observability knowledge via PVC-backed logs, status tracking patterns, and security awareness through NetworkPolicy and InitContainer usage.

## Key Concepts
- **ConfigMaps**: canary-config holds dynamic parameters (targetImage, canaryPercent); canary-status records rollout history and last-promotion timestamps
- **CronJob**: Provides periodic reconciliation that checks configuration and triggers conditional updates
- **Deployment RollingUpdate**: maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0 ensures controlled, non-disruptive updates
- **Traffic Shifting**: Routes a configurable percentage of traffic to canary pods for gradual exposure
- **InitContainer**: Ensures jq availability for JSON parsing within the job
- **Probes**: Readiness gates ensure traffic only reaches healthy pods; liveness checks restart unhealthy instances
- **NetworkPolicy**: Restricts namespace egress to approved registries only
- **PVC**: Persists job logs for debugging and audit trails

## Trade-offs
- **Pros**: Fully Kubernetes-native with no external dependencies; ConfigMap history provides auditability; automatic promotion reduces manual intervention
- **Cons**: Namespace-scoped only; NetworkPolicy requires CNI support; jq dependency increases image size; ConfigMaps lack built-in validation

The Job should gzip rotated logs, prune files older than retention, and post a summary to http://log-portal:8080/api/v1/retention-summary. InitContainer installs gzip/pigz, use a Secret for API creds, a TLS sidecar for cert rotation, with readiness/liveness probes and a network policy restricting egress to the portal. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and explain how you'd test rollbacks?
questions.answer: Implement a CronJob named log-cleaner in namespace analytics that runs hourly, mounts the PVC at /logs/app, reads retention days from log-retention ConfigMap, compresses rotated logs with gzip, prunes
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a maintenance task that runs in Kubernetes using CronJob, PVCs, and ConfigMaps. It also covers secrets, sidecars, probes, and egress constraints in a realistic operational scenario.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes CronJob, PersistentVolumeClaim, ConfigMap, Secret
- InitContainer, TLS sidecar, probes, resource requests
- NetworkPolicy, rollback strategy

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: log-cleaner
  namespace: analytics
spec:
  schedule: '0 * * * *'
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: cleaner
            image: registry.example.com/log-cleaner:latest
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs/app
            envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: log-retention
          initContainers:
          - name: tools
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'apk add --no-cache gzip pigz curl']
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify that log files are pruned and compressed under load?
- How would you rollback a CronJob update and validate it touched no data loss?

The proxy forwards to app-service only when the flag is true; otherwise returns 503. Include an InitContainer to install jq, a templated nginx.conf, a TLS rotation sidecar, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to app-service. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: config-driven behavior, InitContainer use, in-cluster TLS rotation, and precise networking controls with NetworkPolicy.

## Key Concepts

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: feature-proxy
  namespace: ckad-demo
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: render-nginx
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh", "-c", "jq ... && render-nginx-conf.sh"]
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:alpine
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
              subPath: nginx.conf
      volumes:
        - name: config
          emptyDir: {}
```

## Follow-up Questions

Batch results using BATCH_SIZE from audit-config ConfigMap, and POST a summary to http://audit-portal:8080/api/v1/audit every batch. Include an InitContainer to install kubectl and yamllint, a Secret for API creds, a TLS sidecar for rotation, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. questions.explanation:
Why This Is Asked
- Tests practical CKAD skills: PVC-based file intake, in-cluster validation, batch processing, and external posting. Includes init tooling, secret handling, TLS rotation, and network policies.

Key Concepts
- PVC-based file discovery in a Deployment
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- kubectl dry-run validation and yamllint
- Batch processing controlled by a ConfigMap value
- Secrets for API credentials and TLS sidecar rotation
- NetworkPolicy scoping to internal endpoints
- Rollback and observability strategies

Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton for the auditor Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: auditor
  namespace: audit
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: auditor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: auditor
    spec:
      volumes:
        - name: audits
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: audits-pvc
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: audit-config
      initContainers:
        - name: prep-tools
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache kubectl yamllint curl; sleep 0"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: audits
              mountPath: /audits
      containers:
        - name: auditor
          image: registry.example/auditor:latest
          volumeMounts:
            - name: audits
              mountPath: /audits
            - name: config
              mountPath: /etc/audit
          env:
            - name: AUDIT_ENDPOINT
              value: "http://audit-portal:8080/api/v1/audit"
          readinessProbe: {"tcpSocket": {"port": 8080}, "initialDelaySeconds": 15, "periodSeconds": 10}
          livenessProbe: {"tcpSocket": {"port": 8080}, "initialDelaySeconds": 30, "periodSeconds": 20}
      tolerations: []
```

Follow-up Questions
- How would you test under load and simulate a rollback?
- What metrics and logs would you collect to verify correctness?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace database-migration CronJob that applies SQL scripts stored in a ConfigMap to a target database exposed as a Service in the same namespace. Use a Secret for DB credentials, log output to a PVC at /logs, and persist last-run state in a namespace ConfigMap named migration-status. Include an InitContainer to install psql and readiness/liveness probes. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: A robust CKAD approach: a CronJob scheduled every 6h runs a Job that mounts /migrations from a ConfigMap, DB creds from a Secret, and connects to the DB via DB_SERVICE in the namespace. InitContainer
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests cross-resource coordination: ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, InitContainer tooling, and networking.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob and Job wiring; - ConfigMap and Secret usage; - InitContainer provisioning; - Persistent logs via PVC; - Readiness/Liveness Probes; - Namespace NetworkPolicy; - Idempotent migrations.

## Code Example
```yaml
# minimal CronJob skeleton
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-migrate
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: migrate
            image: your-migrate-image:latest
            volumeMounts:
            - name: migrations
              mountPath: /migrations
            env:
            - name: DB_SERVICE
              value: "dbService"
            - name: DB_NAME
              value: "appdb"
          initContainers:
          - name: install-tools
            image: appropriate/curl:jq
            command: ["sh","-c","apt-get update && apt-get install -y psql"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          volumes:
          - name: migrations
            configMap:
              name: migration-scripts
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: migration-logs
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you audit migrations across namespaces? 
- How to handle conflicting migrations across clusters?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace image-policy scanner: a Deployment named image-scan-agent that runs in a single namespace, mounts a PVC at /scans, and uses a ConfigMap image-policy-config with two keys: ALLOWED_REGISTRIES and DISALLOWED_TAGS. Include an InitContainer to install kubectl and jq, and a small script that lists all Deployments in the namespace, extracts container images, and flags any image whose tag is latest or not in an ALLOWED_REGISTRIES; write a digest.json summary to /scans each run. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
Deploy a single Deployment in the target namespace with an InitContainer that installs kubectl and jq, mount a PVC at /scans, and a script that enumerates all Deployments via kubectl, parses container images with jq, and validates each image against the ALLOWED_REGISTRIES and DISALLOWED_TAGS policies defined in the image-policy-config ConfigMap. Flag any image whose tag is 'latest' or originates from a registry not on the allowlist. Write a digest.json summary to /scans on each run.

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: image-policy-config
  namespace: example-ns
data:
  ALLOWED_REGISTRIES: "docker.io,ghcr.io,gcr.io"
  DISALLOWED_TAGS: "latest,dev,test"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: image-scan-pvc
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: image-scan-agent
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: image-scan-agent
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: image-scan-agent
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: tools
          image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
          command: ["sh", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              apt-get update && apt-get install -y jq curl
              curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
              chmod +x kubectl
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
      containers:
        - name: scanner
          image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
          command: ["sh", "/scans/scan.sh"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: scans
              mountPath: /scans
            - name: policy
              mountPath: /config
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
          env:
            - name: PATH
              value: "/tools:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"
      volumes:
        - name: scans
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: image-scan-pvc
        - name: policy
          configMap:
            name: image-policy-config
        - name: tools
          emptyDir: {}
```

```bash
#!/bin/sh
set -e

POLICY_FILE="/config/image-policy-config"
ALLOWED_REGISTRIES=$(grep ALLOWED_REGISTRIES "$POLICY_FILE" | cut -d'=' -f2)
DISALLOWED_TAGS=$(grep DISALLOWED_TAGS "$POLICY_FILE" | cut -d'=' -f2)
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

results={"timestamp":"$TIMESTAMP","namespace":"$TARGET_NAMESPACE","violations":[]}

for deploy in $(kubectl get deployments -n "$TARGET_NAMESPACE" -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
  for img in $(kubectl get deployment "$deploy" -n "$TARGET_NAMESPACE" -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}'); do
    tag=$(echo "$img" | rev | cut -d':' -f1 | rev)
    registry=$(echo "$img" | cut -d'/' -f1)
    
    if echo "$DISALLOWED_TAGS" | grep -qw "$tag"; then
      results=$(echo "$results" | jq ".violations += [{\"deployment\":\"$deploy\",\"image\":\"$img\",\"reason\":\"disallowed_tag\",\"tag\":\"$tag\"}]")
    elif ! echo "$ALLOWED_REGISTRIES" | grep -qw "$registry"; then
      results=$(echo "$results" | jq ".violations += [{\"deployment\":\"$deploy\",\"image\":\"$img\",\"reason\":\"unauthorized_registry\",\"registry\":\"$registry\"}]")
    fi
  done
done

echo "$results" > /scans/digest.json
```

**Trade-offs:**

- **CronJob vs Deployment**: This Deployment runs once and exits; a CronJob is better suited for periodic scanning without manual cleanup.
- **Permission scope**: Requires cluster-wide list Deployments or a RoleBinding per namespace to function correctly.
- **Real-time enforcement**: This approach is audit-only; admission controllers such as OPA Gatekeeper enforce policy at deploy time.
- **Tooling overhead**: The InitContainer adds startup latency; pre-baking tools into the image is faster but less flexible.
- **Scalability**: Running one agent per namespace scales poorly; a centralized scanner with aggregated reporting is more efficient.
- **Tag parsing**: Using `cut -d':' -f1` assumes a single colon separator; registry ports (e.g., `localhost:5000/image:tag`) may cause parsing errors. Consider using more robust image parsing logic in production.
- **Volume lifecycle**: The PVC persists scan history but requires cleanup policy; otherwise, storage grows unbounded over time.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question tests core CKAD competencies: namespace-scoped tooling, PersistentVolume usage, InitContainers for environment preparation, ConfigMap-driven configuration, and scripting with kubectl and jq. It evaluates practical policy enforcement skills without relying on external services or admission webhooks.

## Key Concepts

- **Namespace-scoped resources**: Deployments and PVCs bound to a single namespace, ensuring isolation between scanning agents.
- **InitContainer pattern**: Installing tools (kubectl, jq) at startup before the main container executes.
- **ConfigMap-driven policy**: Externalizing ALLOWED_REGISTRIES and DISALLOWED_TAGS enables runtime policy updates without rebuilding the container image.
- **JSONPath expressions**: Efficiently extracting container images from Deployment specs without full JSON parsing.
- **PersistentVolumeClaims**: Providing durable storage for scan results that survive container restarts.

  Agent --> Init[InitContainer: install kubectl/jq]
  Agent --> Script[Script: list Deployments, extract images]
  Script --> Digest[Digest: /scans/digest.json]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace config-reloader: a Deployment that mounts a PVC at /configs with YAML files, validates them in an InitContainer using yamllint, and signals the main server to reload configuration when a YAML changes using a simple sidecar that touches a shared /reload/trigger file. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton, include readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to internal endpoints. Discuss trade-offs?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to coordinate InitContainers, sidecars, and config-driven apps in CKAD. Ensures proper volume mounting, file-based reloading semantics, and safe network posture.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainer: pre-flight yamllint validation of /configs
- Config mounting: PVC-backed /configs shared with main container and sidecar
- Sidecar watcher: detects changes and triggers in-app reload via /reload/trigger
- Readiness/Liveness probes: ensure proper pod health
- Namespace NetworkPolicy: restricts egress to internal services only

## Code Example
```yaml
# minimal skeleton (Deployment)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: config-reloader
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: config-reloader
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: lint-configs
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "yamllint /configs/**/*.yaml || exit 1"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /configs
      containers:
      - name: main-app
        image: myapp:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /configs
        - name: reload-volume
          mountPath: /reload
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
        env:
        - name: RELOAD_PATH
          value: /reload/trigger
      - name: reload-watcher
        image: alpine:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "inotifywait -m /configs -e modify,create -e moved_to | while read; do touch /reload/trigger; done"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /configs
        - name: reload-volume
          mountPath: /reload
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: config-pvc
      - name: reload-volume
        emptyDir: {}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you add multi-namespace isolation for this pattern?
- What happens on YAML syntax error after initial validation?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[InitContainer: lint] --> B[Main App: /configs]
  B --> C[Sidecar: watch /configs]
  C --> D[Touch: /reload/trigger]
  D --> B

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to compose a CKAD-ready, namespace-scoped data-forwarding pattern using PVC, ConfigMap, Secret, InitContainer, and a TLS-sidecar while enforcing NetworkPolicy. Beginner level focuses on wiring components and basic RBAC considerations; ensures understanding of trade-offs between latency, throughput, and security.

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: event-forwarder
  namespace: your-namespace
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: event-forwarder
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: event-forwarder
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: tools
          image: alpine:3.17
          command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: events
              mountPath: /var/log/events
      containers:
        - name: forwarder
          image: your-registry/event-forwarder:latest
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: forward-config
          volumeMounts:
            - name: events
              mountPath: /var/log/events
        - name: tls-sidecar
          image: your-registry/tls-sidecar:latest
          # TLS rotation logic
      volumes:
        - name: events
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: events-pvc
```

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace config-schema-validator: a CronJob in the target namespace that runs every 7 minutes to fetch a JSON Schema URL from ConfigMap validation-config, validate all ConfigMaps in the namespace with jq, and POST a summary to http://validation-portal:8080/api/v1/validate. InitContainer installs curl/jq; Secret stores API creds; logs to PVC /logs; persist status in ConfigMap validation-status; Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the schema URL and portal. Minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Implement a CronJob in the target namespace with an InitContainer that installs curl and jq. The job reads the JSON Schema URL from the validation-config ConfigMap, enumerates all ConfigMaps in the namespace, validates each against the schema using jq, and POSTs a summary to http://validation-portal:8080/api/v1/validate. API credentials are stored in a Secret, logs are written to a PVC mounted at /logs, and validation status is persisted in the validation-status ConfigMap. A Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to only the schema URL and the validation portal.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates CKAD proficiency in extending namespace-centric validation using core Kubernetes primitives: ConfigMaps, Secrets, CronJobs, InitContainers, PVCs, and NetworkPolicies. It also tests knowledge of jq for in-cluster JSON Schema validation and idempotent status updates.

## Key Concepts

- **Namespace-scoped configuration**: All resources operate within a single namespace boundary
- **CronJob with InitContainer tooling**: InitContainer prepares the environment (curl/jq) before the main validation container runs
- **JSON Schema validation using jq**: jq processes ConfigMap data against the fetched schema
- **PVC-backed logging**: Persistent storage preserves logs across job executions
- **NetworkPolicy egress control**: Restricts outbound traffic to only approved endpoints
- **Status persistence in ConfigMap**: Validation results are stored for auditing and downstream consumption

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: ns-config-schema-validator
  namespace: <target-namespace>
spec:
  schedule: "*/7 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: busybox
            command: ["sh", "-c", "wget -O /usr/local/bin/jq https://github.com/jqlang/jq/releases/download/jq-1.7/jq-linux64 && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/jq"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /usr/local/bin
          containers:
          - name: validator
            image: curlimages/curl:latest
            env:
            - name: API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: validation-creds
                  key: api-key
            command: ["sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |
              SCHEMA_URL=$(kubectl get configmap validation-config -o jsonpath='{.data.schema-url}')
              curl -s $SCHEMA_URL -o /logs/schema.json
              for cm in $(kubectl get configmaps -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
                kubectl get configmap $cm -o json | jq -s ".[0] * {data: .[1] | getpath($cm | split(\"-\")[1:] | map(.))}" /logs/schema.json > /logs/validation-$cm.json || true
              done
              curl -X POST http://validation-portal:8080/api/v1/validate -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" --data-binary @/logs/summary.json
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /usr/local/bin
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: validation-logs
          - name: tools
            emptyDir: {}
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Trade-offs

| Aspect | Trade-off |
|--------|-----------|
| **InitContainer vs. Custom Image** | InitContainer adds startup latency but keeps the main image lightweight and avoids maintenance of a custom image. |
| **jq Validation vs. Dedicated Validator** | jq is lightweight and universally available, but lacks full JSON Schema draft support. A dedicated validator (e.g., kubeconform) offers better accuracy at the cost of added complexity. |
| **PVC for Logs** | Provides durability and debugging capability but introduces storage overhead and potential bottlenecks in high-frequency runs. |
| **NetworkPolicy Egress** | Restricts access to only required endpoints, enhancing security but requires careful planning of all dependencies. |
| **ConfigMap Status vs. Custom Resource** | ConfigMap is simpler and requires no CRD management, but lacks built-in versioning and conflict resolution. |

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace image-scan pipeline: a Deployment watches a PVC-mounted /manifests directory containing Kubernetes manifest YAMLs, extracts container images, runs Trivy against a local DB, batches results per BATCH_SIZE from a ConfigMap named scan-config, and POSTs a summary to http://scan-portal:8080/api/v1/summary. Include InitContainer to install Trivy, a Secret for API creds, a TLS sidecar, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Design a per-namespace image-scan pipeline: a Deployment watches a PVC /manifests, uses an InitContainer to install Trivy, scans images extracted from YAMLs, batches by BATCH_SIZE from ConfigMap scan-
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD ability to combine manifest sourcing, container image scanning, ConfigMap-driven batching, and secure network boundaries in a single namespace.

## Key Concepts
- Deployment with PVC-backed manifests
- InitContainer installation of tools (Trivy)
- ConfigMap as runtime parameter (BATCH_SIZE)
- Secret for API credentials and TLS sidecar
- NetworkPolicy scoping to internal endpoint
- Readiness/Liveness probes

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: image-scan
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: image-scan
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: image-scan
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: busybox:stable
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache trivy; sleep 1"]
      containers:
      - name: scanner
        image: myregistry/scan-runner:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /manifests
      volumes:
      - name: manifests
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: manifests-pvc
```

flowchart TD

  S --> P[Post summary to scan-portal]
  P --> N[Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress]

Include an InitContainer to install jq, a Secret for API credentials, a TLS cert rotation sidecar, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to combine PVC-backed data, in-pod streaming, simple aggregation, and exposing a small HTTP endpoint with Basic Kubernetes primitives.

## Key Concepts
- Tail -F in a container and in-memory rolling window
- PVC data access and volume mounts
- Writing results to a mounted path
- Lightweight HTTP server for status
- InitContainers, Secrets, TLS sidecar, probes, and NetworkPolicy

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cost-collector
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cost-collector
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: alpine:3.19
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache jq curl && sleep 0"]
      containers:
      - name: collector
        image: alpine:3.19
        volumeMounts:
        - name: metrics
          mountPath: /mnt/metrics
        - name: out
          mountPath: /summaries
        command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -F /mnt/metrics/usage.jsonl | ./process-and-serve.sh"]
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: metrics
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: metrics-pvc
      - name: out
        emptyDir: {}
      # TLS sidecar and service account permissions omitted for brevity
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale to multiple namespaces? 
- How would you secure the HTTP server and rotate credentials?

flowchart TD
