  B --> C[Tail + rolling-sum]
  C --> D[Write /summaries/latest.json]
  B --> E[HTTP server on 8080]
  E --> F[TLS sidecar rotates certs]
  G[NetworkPolicy: restrict egress to internal endpoint] --> H[Internal endpoint]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace outbound allowlist manager: a CronJob in each namespace reads allowedHosts from a ConfigMap, resolves to IPs, and updates a namespace NetworkPolicy to permit egress only to those CIDRs on port 443. A separate Job tests reachability from pods and stores results to a PVC at /logs. InitContainer installs curl; include readiness/liveness probes and a default-deny egress. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
## Approach

Deploy a per-namespace CronJob that reads `allowedHosts` from a ConfigMap, resolves hostnames to IP addresses, and creates or updates a namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy permitting egress only to those resolved CIDRs on port 443.

A separate Job runs reachability tests from pods and persists results to a PVC at `/logs`. An InitContainer ensures curl is available, readiness and liveness probes are configured for the test Job, and a default-deny egress policy provides full isolation.

## Minimal YAML Skeleton

```yaml
---
# Default-deny egress for the namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-egress
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
---
# ConfigMap holding the allowlist
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: allowed-hosts
  namespace: example-ns
data:
  allowedHosts: |
    api.example.com
    internal.example.org
---
# CronJob: resolves hosts and updates NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: update-egress-allowlist
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"  # Every 6 hours
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: egress-manager-sa
          containers:
          - name: resolver
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              set -e
              HOSTS=$(cat /config/allowedHosts)
              for HOST in $HOSTS; do
                IP=$(nslookup "$HOST" 2>/dev/null | grep '^Address:' | tail -1 | awk '{print $2}')
                if [ -n "$IP" ]; then
                  echo "$IP/32"
                fi
              done > /tmp/cidrs.txt
              
              CIDRS=$(tr '\n' ',' < /tmp/cidrs.txt | sed 's/,$//')
              
              kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
              apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
              kind: NetworkPolicy
              metadata:
                name: outbound-allowlist
                namespace: example-ns
              spec:
                podSelector: {}
                policyTypes:
                - Egress
                egress:
                - to:
                  - namespaceSelector: {}
                  ports:
                  - protocol: TCP
                    port: 53
                  - protocol: UDP
                    port: 53
                - to:
                  - ipBlock:
                      cidr: 169.254.169.254/32
                  ports:
                  - protocol: TCP
                    port: 80
                - to:
                  - ipBlock:
                      cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
                  ports:
                  - protocol: TCP
                    port: 443
              EOF
            volumeMounts:
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
          volumes:
          - name: config
            configMap:
              name: allowed-hosts
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
# Job: tests reachability and logs to PVC
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: egress-reachability-test
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 300
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: curl-installer
        image: curlimages/curl:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'curl ready'"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: reachability-test
        image: curlimages/curl:latest
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - |
          HOSTS=$(cat /config/allowedHosts)
          for HOST in $HOSTS; do
            STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 "https://$HOST" || echo "000")
            echo "$(date -Iseconds),$HOST,$STATUS" >> /logs/reachability.log
          done
          touch /ready
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command: ["cat", "/ready"]
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          tcpSocket:
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 30
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        - name: config
          mountPath: /config
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: egress-logs-pvc
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: allowed-hosts
      restartPolicy: Never
---
# PVC for storing test logs
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: egress-logs-pvc
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
# RBAC: ServiceAccount and Role for policy management
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: egress-manager-sa
  namespace: example-ns
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: networkpolicy-manager
  namespace: example-ns
rules:
- apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["networkpolicies"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: networkpolicy-manager-binding
  namespace: example-ns
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: networkpolicy-manager
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: egress-manager-sa
  namespace: example-ns
```

## Trade-offs

| Aspect | Consideration |
|--------|----------------|
| **DNS Drift** | Resolved IPs can become stale if hostnames point to dynamic endpoints. Shortening the CronJob interval reduces drift but increases API server load. |
| **IPBlock Limitations** | NetworkPolicy `ipBlock` only supports CIDRs, not hostnames. Multiple IPs per hostname require multiple rules, complicating policy management. |
| **Egress and `except`** | Combining `except` with `0.0.0.0/0` can be error-prone; ensure precise CIDR exclusions to avoid unintended gaps in allowlisting. |
| **Default-Deny Scope** | Namespace-wide default-deny is safe but requires explicit allowlisting of DNS (53/UDP) and cloud metadata services (169.254.169.254). |
| **CNI Compatibility** | Some CNIs (e.g., older Calico, Azure CNI) have partial NetworkPolicy support. Always validate egress enforcement in your environment. |
| **InitContainer Overhead** | Using an InitContainer to install curl adds startup latency. Pre-baking curl into the image eliminates this but increases image size. |
| **PVC for Logs** | A shared PVC works for single-job logging but requires careful access mode selection. For multi-pod scenarios, consider a centralized log aggregator such as Loki or Elasticsearch. |
| **RBAC Blast Radius** | Granting write access to NetworkPolicy within the namespace is correctly scoped but should be audited regularly to prevent privilege escalation. |
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question evaluates the ability to translate dynamic allowlists into enforced Kubernetes NetworkPolicy rules while maintaining per-namespace isolation. It combines ConfigMap-driven configuration, DNS resolution, RBAC scoping, and observability—all core CKAD competencies.

## Key Concepts

- **NetworkPolicy**: Enforces egress rules at the pod level using `ipBlock` selectors. Note that egress rules require CNI support for enforcement.
- **ConfigMap**: Serves as the externalized allowlist store, allowing updates without pod or job restarts.
- **CronJob**: Periodically reconciles the allowlist state with the active NetworkPolicy, acting as a controller loop.
- **InitContainer**: Ensures required tools are available before the main container starts, though using a pre-built image with curl is simpler.
- **PVC**: Provides persistent storage for test results, enabling post-job analysis and debugging.

## Common Pitfalls

- Forgetting DNS egress rules causes complete name resolution failure
- Using `0.0.0.0/0` with broad `except` blocks can create confusing policies
- Resolving hostnames to IPs at apply time introduces a lag between DNS changes and policy updates
- Not all CNIs fully support egress policies; test enforcement in your target environment

## Production Considerations

- Add error handling and alerting for resolution failures
- Consider using a mutating webhook or operator for more robust reconciliation
- Implement metric export (Prometheus) for monitoring resolution success rates
- Add retry logic with exponential backoff for transient network failures
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[ConfigMap] --> B[CronJob]
  B --> C[NetworkPolicy]
  C --> D[Pods]
  D --> E[Logs PVC]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace data-collection and delivery pipeline: a Deployment named data-collector reads files from a namespace-scoped PVC mounted at /data, computes a rolling summary every N files, and a CronJob in the same namespace triggers every 5 minutes to POST the latest summary to an external API using a Bearer token from a Secret, while persisting raw/summary logs to a PVC at /logs. Use an InitContainer to install curl, include readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the external API endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
This design implements a per-namespace data pipeline with three components:

1. **Deployment (data-collector)**: Mounts a namespace-scoped PVC at `/data`, uses an InitContainer to install curl, computes a rolling summary every N files, and persists both raw and summary logs to a PVC at `/logs`. Includes readiness and liveness probes.

2. **CronJob**: Runs every 5 minutes, reads the latest summary from the log PVC, and POSTs it to an external API using a Bearer token retrieved from a Secret.

3. **NetworkPolicy**: Restricts egress traffic to the external API endpoint only.

**YAML Skeleton:**

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: data-pvc
  namespace: example
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: logs-pvc
  namespace: example
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 500Mi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-credentials
  namespace: example
type: Opaque
stringData:
  bearer-token: "<token>"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: data-collector
  namespace: example
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: data-collector
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: data-collector
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: curl-installer
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ['sh', '-c', 'apk add --no-cache curl || apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl']
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
      containers:
      - name: collector
        image: your-collector-image:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
      volumes:
      - name: data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: data-pvc
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: logs-pvc
---
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: summary-sender
  namespace: example
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: sender
            image: curlimages/curl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              TOKEN=$(cat /secrets/bearer-token)
              curl -X POST https://api.example.com/summary \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
                -d @$(ls -t /logs/summary*.json | head -1)
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            - name: secrets
              mountPath: /secrets
              readOnly: true
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          - name: secrets
            secret:
              secretName: api-credentials
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: api-egress-only
  namespace: example
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: external-api
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

**Trade-offs:**

| Aspect | Trade-off |
|--------|------------|
| **InitContainer** | Adds startup latency but ensures tooling availability without bloating the main image |
| **Shared PVC** | Simple architecture but creates coupling; consider separate PVCs for data versus logs |
| **CronJob concurrency** | `concurrencyPolicy: Forbid` prevents overlapping runs but may cause missed executions under load |
| **Bearer token in Secret** | More secure than hardcoding, but requires a rotation strategy |
| **NetworkPolicy** | Enforces strict egress controls but requires careful CIDR or namespace labeling |
| **PVC access modes** | `ReadWriteOnce` limits to single-node; `ReadWriteMany` may be unavailable on many cloud providers |
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question assesses practical CKAD competencies including PersistentVolumes, InitContainers, Jobs/CronJobs, Secrets, NetworkPolicies, and observability—skills essential for production Kubernetes workloads.

## Key Concepts

- **PVC mounting and volume permissions**: Namespace-scoped storage with appropriate access modes
- **InitContainer tooling**: Pre-installing dependencies (curl) without inflating the main container image
- **Job scheduling and idempotency**: CronJobs running every 5 minutes with collision prevention
- **NetworkPolicy egress controls**: Restricting traffic to least-privilege access patterns
- **Secret management**: Secure credential handling via Kubernetes Secrets
- **Health probes**: Ensuring application availability and reliability through readiness and liveness checks

## Common Pitfalls

- Forgetting `concurrencyPolicy` can lead to overlapping job executions
- InitContainer failures block the main container; ensure reliable installation commands
- NetworkPolicy egress rules must correctly identify targets; misconfiguration blocks all traffic
- PVC access mode mismatches cause pod scheduling failures

  API --> Sec[Secret: api-token]
  NP[NetworkPolicy] --> API

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD patterns: watch resources in a namespace, ConfigMap/Secret usage, PVC-backed logs, InitContainer tooling, readiness/liveness, and a restrictive NetworkPolicy. It exercises multi-resource coordination and security boundaries.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped controllers and watches
- ConfigMap updates and digest semantics
- Secrets for credentials and secure API calls
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- Probes for reliability
- PVC-based logging
- Namespace NetworkPolicy egress controls

## Code Example
```yaml
# minimal skeleton: Deployment (audit-logger), ConfigMap (audit-digest), Secret (audit-token), PVC (audit-logs), NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: audit-logger
  namespace: target-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: audit-logger
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: audit-logger
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: logger
        image: your-registry/audit-logger:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        env:
        - name: AUDIT_ENDPOINT
          value: "https://audit-portal.example/api/v1/heartbeat"
        - name: AUDIT_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: audit-token
              key: token
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command: ["test", "-f", "/logs/ready"]
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: audit-logs
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you RBAC-scopes affect the watch and updates to the ConfigMap?
- What would you monitor to handle digest collisions or out-of-order heartbeats?
- How would you test this in a CI pipeline?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, implement per-namespace resource governance: a Namespace ResourceQuota to cap total pod memory and CPU, a corresponding LimitRange for per-pod requests/limits, and a Deployment in that namespace that creates pods hitting the quota. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: First, create a Namespace for isolation. Then apply a ResourceQuota with hard limits (cpu: "4", memory: "8Gi", pods: "20"). Next, configure a LimitRange to enforce default requests/limits for containers within that namespace. Finally, deploy a Deployment whose pod specs request resources within the quota bounds. Validate with `kubectl describe resourcequota` and `kubectl describe limitrange`. Trade-offs include reduced flexibility (devs must specify requests/limits), potential underutilization if quotas are misconfigured, and added complexity when debugging scheduling failures.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical knowledge of namespace-scoped governance primitives and their impact on scheduling, pod density, and autoscaler behavior.

## Key Concepts
- **ResourceQuota** enforces hard caps on total namespace resource consumption (CPU, memory, pod count, etc.)
- **LimitRange** sets defaults and constraints for individual pod/container resource requests and limits
- Quota enforcement affects scheduler decisions, pod scheduling failures, and autoscaler calculations
- Use `kubectl describe resourcequota <name> --namespace=<ns>` and `kubectl top pod` to monitor usage

## Code Example

```yaml
# Namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: quota-demo
---
# ResourceQuota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: ns-quota
  namespace: quota-demo
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: "16Gi"
    pods: "20"
---
# LimitRange
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: ns-limits
  namespace: quota-demo
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 512Mi
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: 250m
      memory: 256Mi
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 128Mi
---
# Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: quota-demo-app
  namespace: quota-demo
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 128Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi
```

## Trade-offs
- **Flexibility vs. Control**: Quotas prevent resource hoarding but require developers to specify requests/limits, increasing YAML complexity
- **Utilization Risk**: Overly restrictive quotas lead to pod scheduling failures; overly generous quotas waste resources
- **Debugging Overhead**: Quota-induced failures (e.g., "pods quota exceeded") add a layer of complexity to troubleshooting
- **Autoscaler Interaction**: VPA/HPA behavior changes when quotas constrain available headroom
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Create Namespace] --> B[Apply ResourceQuota]
  B --> C[Apply LimitRange]
  C --> D[Deploy App]
  D --> E[Verify]

questions.question: In namespace hardening, design a per-namespace audit CronJob that scans all Deployments for containers with securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true or readOnlyRootFilesystem != true, and writes a remediation plan to a PVC and emits a summary to a service at http://audit-portal:8080/api/v1/audit. Include an InitContainer to install jq, a ConfigMap with the audit script, a Secret for API creds, a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint, and describe how you'd verify and rollback?
questions.answer: Deploy a CronJob named security-audit in the target namespace every 2 minutes. InitContainer installs jq, main script lists Deployments, checks containers’ securityContext.runAsNonRoot and readOnlyRoo
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to implement namespace-scoped security audits using standard CKAD patterns: CronJob scheduling, InitContainer tooling, RBAC, ConfigMaps for scripts, and NetworkPolicies. It also covers end-to-end verification and rollback.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob in Kubernetes
- InitContainer installation steps (jq)
- Security context checks: runAsNonRoot and readOnlyRootFilesystem
- ConfigMap/script for audit logic
- PVC-backed output and API POST
- Namespace NetworkPolicy egress controls

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton snippet for the CronJob (not complete)
apiVersion: batch/v1beta1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: security-audit
  namespace: hardening
spec:
  schedule: "*/2 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: audit-sa
          initContainers:
            - name: install-tools
              image: alpine:3.18
              command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache jq curl;"]
          containers:
            - name: auditor
              image: auditor:latest
              volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: /audits
                  name: audit-work
          volumes:
            - name: audit-work
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: audit-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test this under load and simulate noncompliant deployments?
- How would you rollback a faulty remediation manifest without data loss?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace secret-rotation agent that runs as a Deployment in every namespace. It polls a central rotation API every 5 minutes for TLS certs, using a Token from a Secret, writes the certificate into a Secret named app-tls, updates a ConfigMap with lastRotation, and triggers a rolling restart of dependent Deployments. Logs go to a PVC at /logs; InitContainer installs curl; include readiness/liveness probes; a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the rotation API. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Approach: In each namespace deploy a small agent (Deployment) that curl-fetches new TLS certs from the central rotation API every 5m using a token from a Secret; write cert to a Secret named app-tls v
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests per-namespace secret management, rotation workflow, and safe rollouts in CKAD terms. Requires RBAC awareness, InitContainers, and network isolation.

## Key Concepts
- Secret rotation via API and patch
- ConfigMap as state store for rotation timestamps
- Rollout restarts triggered by annotation or config change
- InitContainer for tooling installation
- Namespace NetworkPolicy for egress control

## Code Example
```yaml
# Skeleton: Deployment (agent) in a namespace
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: secret-rotator
  namespace: <ns>
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: installer
        image: curl:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl && mkdir -p /logs"]
      containers:
      - name: rotator
        image: secret-rotator:latest
        env:
        - name: ROTATION_API
          value: https://rotation-api.local/rotate
        - name: API_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: rotation-token
              key: token
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: logs
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you ensure idempotence of a rotation? 
- What RBAC rules would you apply for the agent? 
- How would you handle API failures or partial rotations?

questions.question: In a CKAD scenario, you need to isolate a payment microservice from other pods while allowing only approved traffic within namespace 'finance'. The Deployment 'payment' has 3 replicas. Write a NetworkPolicy that (a) allows Ingress to payment only from pods labeled app=frontend in the same namespace, (b) allows Egress from payment only to pods labeled app=db on port 5432 and to pods labeled app=cache on port 6379. Provide the minimal policy manifest and describe how to validate under load?
questions.answer: Create a NetworkPolicy named payment-allow in namespace finance selecting app=payment; ingress only from pods labeled app=frontend in the same namespace; egress only to pods labeled app=db on port 543
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical use of NetworkPolicy to enforce zero-trust interactions between microservices and their dependents, a common production pattern.

## Key Concepts
- NetworkPolicy basics: podSelector, ingress/egress, policyTypes
- namespace-scoped rules and label selectors
- Egress to specific app Pods on defined ports
- Validation: simulate allowed and blocked traffic, inspect policy state

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: payment-allow
  namespace: finance
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payment
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: frontend
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: db
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: cache
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 6379
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt if payment also needs cross-namespace database access?
- What runtime and network-layer observability would you enable to detect policy violations?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace metrics-aggregator: a Deployment in each namespace queries metrics.k8s.io for Pods in that namespace, batches CPU/memory by app label over a WINDOW from metrics-config, and POSTs a digest to https://metrics-portal/api/v1/ingest using a Bearer token from metrics-portal-secret. Logs go to /logs PVC. InitContainer installs curl/jq; add probes; NetworkPolicy restricts egress to the portal. Provide a skeleton YAML and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
Here's the design:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: metrics-aggregation
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: metrics-config
  namespace: metrics-aggregation
data:
  window: "5m"
  batch-size: "100"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: metrics-portal-secret
  namespace: metrics-aggregation
type: Opaque
stringData:
  bearer-token: "${BEARER_TOKEN}"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: logs-pvc
  namespace: metrics-aggregation
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: metrics-aggregator
  namespace: metrics-aggregation
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: metrics-aggregator
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: metrics-aggregator
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: metrics-aggregator-sa
      initContainers:
      - name: tools-installer
        image: curlimages/curl:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /usr/bin/curl /tools/ && chmod 755 /tools/*"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tools
          mountPath: /tools
      containers:
      - name: aggregator
        image: your-aggregator-image:latest
        env:
        - name: METRICS_WINDOW
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: metrics-config
              key: window
        - name: BEARER_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: metrics-portal-secret
              key: bearer-token
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        - name: tools
          mountPath: /tools
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: logs-pvc
      - name: tools
        emptyDir: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: metrics-aggregator-egress
  namespace: metrics-aggregation
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: metrics-aggregator
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: kube-system
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

**Trade-offs:**

| Aspect | Consideration |
|--------|---------------|
| **InitContainer** | Adds startup latency; for faster boots, bake curl directly into the image instead. |
| **PVC for Logs** | Guarantees durability but introduces storage overhead. Alternatively, stream logs to stdout and collect via a sidecar or node-level log shipper. |
| **Per-Namespace Deployment** | Isolates namespaces cleanly but multiplies resource consumption. A single cluster-scoped deployment with namespace filtering may be more efficient for large clusters. |
| **NetworkPolicy** | Restricts egress to the metrics portal plus kube-system for DNS. Adjust the IP block if the portal has a fixed external address. |
| **Secrets** | Storing the Bearer token in a Secret is acceptable when paired with a restricted ServiceAccount. For stronger security, use short-lived tokens via the TokenRequest API. |
| **Probes** | Readiness gates traffic until the application is prepared; liveness catches hung processes but may cause unnecessary restarts if thresholds are too aggressive. |

**Security:** The ServiceAccount (`metrics-aggregator-sa`) requires `get` permission on `pods.metrics.k8s.io` scoped to its own namespace only."
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question assesses the ability to design per-namespace tooling that leverages core Kubernetes primitives without requiring cluster-scoped operators. It validates understanding of security boundaries, namespace isolation, and storage patterns in a cloud-native context.

## Key Concepts Tested

- **Metrics API access and per-namespace RBAC**: Querying `metrics.k8s.io` requires a ClusterRoleBinding or Role with `get` permissions on `pods.metrics.k8s.io` scoped to the target namespace.
- **ConfigMap-driven configuration**: Externalizing the aggregation window enables runtime adjustments without redeploying.
- **Security hardening**: NetworkPolicy, least-privilege ServiceAccounts, and Secret management demonstrate defense-in-depth thinking.
- **Operational concerns**: PVCs for logs, health probes, and init containers address real-world reliability requirements.

## Common Pitfalls

- Forgetting that `metrics.k8s.io` is a custom API group requiring proper RBAC—not included in default `view`/`edit` ClusterRoles.
- Overly permissive NetworkPolicies that allow all egress instead of targeting specific endpoints.
- Storing secrets as plain environment variables instead of referencing them from a Secret resource.

## Extensions

- Add a CronJob wrapper to schedule aggregation runs rather than running continuously.
- Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures when posting to the portal.
- Consider adding Prometheus metrics to the aggregator itself for observability of the observability tool.

  B --> E[Logs PVC: /logs]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, implement a per-namespace scheduled migration job: a CronJob in each target namespace that runs hourly to migrate ConfigMaps by renaming keys from old-prefix to new-prefix, based on a migration plan stored in a namespace ConfigMap. Use an InitContainer to install yq, read the plan, update all relevant ConfigMaps, and write per-run logs to a PVC at /logs. Post a summary to http://migration-portal:8080/api/v1/migrate using a Secret for API tokens. Include readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the portal. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Propose a per-namespace CronJob that runs hourly, uses an InitContainer to install yq, reads a migration plan from a namespace ConfigMap, migrates all matching ConfigMaps by renaming keys from old-pre
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD proficiency across namespace-scoped automation, ConfigMap manipulation, InitContainers, and secure egress controls in real-world migration workflows.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob scoped to a namespace with per-namespace behavior
- InitContainer to install tooling (yq) and prepare environment
- ConfigMap-driven migration plan and idempotent updates
- PVC-backed logs and a Secret-backed external POST
- Readiness/Liveness probes for resilient Jobs
- Namespace NetworkPolicy to restrict egress to the migration portal

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton: per-namespace CronJob migrating ConfigMaps
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: config-migrate
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: migrate-sa
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: alpine:3.17
            command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache go && go install github.com/mikefarah/yq@latest"]
          containers:
          - name: migrate
            image: my-registry/config-migrate:latest
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: migration-api
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you ensure idempotent migrations across runs?
- How would you handle partial failures or large numbers of ConfigMaps?
- What monitoring would you add to detect drift or failures?

questions.question: In namespace analytics, implement a CronJob named data-migrate that runs hourly to read YAML manifests from a PVC mounted at /manifests, convert them to JSON written to /out, and use a Secret for credentials. Include an InitContainer to install yq/jq, a ConfigMap mounted at /config for options, concurrencyPolicy: Forbid, backoffLimit: 3, successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3, readiness/liveness probes for the pod, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to internal endpoints. Provide a minimal manifest sketch and a brief rollback strategy?
questions.answer: Use a CronJob with `concurrencyPolicy: Forbid` to prevent overlapping executions. Configure `backoffLimit: 3` for retry handling and `successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3` for auditability. Mount the PVC at both `/manifests` (input) and `/out` (output) paths, attach the ConfigMap at `/config` for options, and reference the Secret for credentials. An InitContainer installs `yq` and `jq` to handle YAML-to-JSON conversion. Add readiness and liveness probes to monitor pod health, and apply a NetworkPolicy in the namespace to restrict egress to internal endpoints only.
questions.explanation:
This question assesses CKAD-level ability to compose a batch job with initialization steps, configuration management, and access controls. It also evaluates understanding of idempotence and rollback planning.

**Key Concepts**
- CronJob scheduling and `concurrencyPolicy` for preventing concurrent runs
- InitContainer for dependency installation
- ConfigMap and Secret mounting patterns
- PVC usage across Job pods
- Readiness and Liveness probes for batch workloads
- NetworkPolicy for egress restrictions

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: data-migrate
  namespace: analytics
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  backoffLimit: 3
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: busybox
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'wget -O /usr/local/bin/yq ... && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/yq']
            volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /usr/local/bin
          containers:
          - name: migrate
            image: curlimages/curl
            args: ['sh', '-c', 'yq -o=json /manifests/*.yaml > /out/manifests.json']
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ['test', '-f', '/out/manifests.json']
              initialDelaySeconds: 10
              periodSeconds: 30
            livenessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ['sh', '-c', 'ls /out']
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 60
            volumeMounts:
            - name: manifests
              mountPath: /manifests
            - name: out
              mountPath: /out
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: credentials
          volumes:
          - name: manifests
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: manifest-pvc
          - name: out
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: manifest-pvc
          - name: config
            configMap:
              name: migrate-options
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

**Rollback Strategy**: To rollback, either suspend the CronJob with `kubectl patch cronjob data-migrate -n analytics -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'` or reduce the `successfulJobsHistoryLimit` to 0 to prevent new Jobs from being created.
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[CronJob] --> B[Job]
B --> C[Pod]
C --> D[/manifests (PVC)]
C --> E[/out (PVC)]
C --> F[/config (ConfigMap)]
C --> G[/credentials (Secret)]

questions.question: In namespace ci, a CronJob 'image-build' uses Kaniko to build from /workspace/Dockerfile on a PVC and push to registry.local/ci. Patch the CronJob to mount a ConfigMap at /workspace/config, add a checksum/config/workspace-config annotation to trigger rollouts on config changes, set resource requests/limits, add readinessProbe on /healthz and livenessProbe on /healthz, a preStop sleep 5s, and a PodDisruptionBudget requiring at least 2 healthy pods. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and verification steps?
questions.answer: Patch CronJob to mount /workspace/config ConfigMap, add checksum annotation to PodTemplateSpec, define resources, add readiness/liveness probes targeting /healthz, include a preStopSleep 5s, and creat
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: CronJob usage with Kaniko, PVC-backed build context, ConfigMap-driven config, rollout triggers via checksum, resource budgeting, and health checks. It also covers RBAC, network isolation, and upgrade/downgrade workflows.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob with Kubernetes Jobs and Kaniko
- ConfigMap mounting and rollout triggers via PodTemplate checksum annotation
- Resource requests/limits
- Readiness/Liveness probes and startup behavior
- preStop lifecycle hook
- PodDisruptionBudget
- RBAC: Role/RoleBinding for build operations
- NetworkPolicy isolation

## Code Example
```yaml
# Skeleton: CronJob with Kaniko build from PVC, mounts ConfigMap, and RBAC
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: image-build
  namespace: ci
spec:
  schedule: "*/15 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: kaniko-builder
          containers:
          - name: kaniko
            image: gcr.io/kaniko-project/executor:latest
            # minimal args for Kaniko, build context from /workspace
            args: ["--context", "/workspace", "--destination", "registry.local/ci/${JOB_ID}"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: workspace
              mountPath: /workspace
            ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 10
              periodSeconds: 5
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
            resources:
              requests:
                cpu: "100m"
                memory: "128Mi"
              limits:
                cpu: "500m"
                memory: "512Mi"
          volumes:
          - name: workspace
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: workspace-pvc
          imagePullSecrets:
          - name: regcred
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          # ConfigMap mount for /workspace/config
          volumeMounts:
          - name: workspace-config
            mountPath: /workspace/config
          securityContext: {}
  
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: workspace-config
  namespace: ci
data:
  BUILD_ARGS: "--build-arg FOO=bar"
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate that changes to the ConfigMap trigger a rolling restart without downtime?
- What changes would you make to support multi-namespace isolation and audit logging for builds?

questions.question: In namespace log, implement a per-namespace log-sanitizer: a Deployment 'log-sanitizer' with 2 replicas reading raw JSON logs from PVC '/logs/raw' (log-pvc) and writing sanitized logs to '/logs/processed'. ConfigMap 'sanitizer-config' lists keys to strip (password, token). Use an InitContainer to install jq and a Python sanitizer script; a sidecar 'shipper' tails '/logs/processed' and POSTs to http://log-portal:9200/api/v1/logs. Include readiness /ready, liveness /live, resource requests/limits, preStop sleep, and a PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable: 1. Provide a minimal manifest patch and verification steps?
questions.answer:
Configure a Deployment in the `log` namespace with 2 replicas. Mount PVC `log-pvc` at `/logs/raw` (read-only for the sanitizer container) and `/logs/processed` (read-write for both sanitizer and shipper). The InitContainer installs `jq` and a Python sanitizer script from ConfigMap `sanitizer-config`, which reads raw JSON logs from `/logs/raw`, strips keys listed in the ConfigMap (e.g., `password`, `token`), and writes sanitized logs to `/logs/processed`. The sidecar container `shipper` tails `/logs/processed` and POSTs to `http://log-portal:9200/api/v1/logs`. Include readiness (`/ready`) and liveness (`/live`) probes, resource requests/limits, a `preStop` sleep for graceful shutdown, and a PodDisruptionBudget with `minAvailable: 1`.

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: log-sanitizer
  namespace: log
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: log-sanitizer
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: log-sanitizer
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      initContainers:
        - name: installer
          image: debian:book-slim
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
          args:
            - apt-get update && apt-get install -y jq python3 && cp /scripts/sanitize.py /tools/sanitize.py
          volumeMounts:
            - name: scripts
              mountPath: /scripts
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
      containers:
        - name: sanitizer
          image: debian:book-slim
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          command: ["python3", "/tools/sanitize.py"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: raw-logs
              mountPath: /logs/raw
              readOnly: true
            - name: processed-logs
              mountPath: /logs/processed
            - name: config
              mountPath: /config
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /live
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 256Mi
          lifecycle:
            preStop:
              exec:
                command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 10"]
        - name: shipper
          image: curlimages/curl:latest
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              while true; do
                tail -F /logs/processed/*.json 2>/dev/null | while IFS= read -r line; do
                  curl -s -X POST http://log-portal:9200/api/v1/logs \
                    -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "$line" || true
                done
                sleep 5
              done
          volumeMounts:
            - name: processed-logs
              mountPath: /logs/processed
      volumes:
        - name: raw-logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: log-pvc
        - name: processed-logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: log-pvc
        - name: scripts
          configMap:
            name: sanitizer-config
        - name: tools
          emptyDir: {}
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: sanitizer-config
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: log-sanitizer-pdb
  namespace: log
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: log-sanitizer
```

**Verification Steps:**
1. `kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml`
2. `kubectl get pods -n log -l app=log-sanitizer` — verify 2/2 Running
3. `kubectl describe pdb log-sanitizer-pdb -n log` — confirm minAvailable: 1
4. `kubectl exec <pod> -n log -c installer -- cat /tools/sanitize.py` — verify script installed
5. Write test log: `echo '{"msg":"test","password":"secret"}' > /mnt/logs/raw/test.json`
6. Check processed output for key removal
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests the ability to compose multi-container pod patterns, implement ConfigMap-driven sanitization logic, and ensure per-namespace isolation with a clean rollback strategy.

## Key Concepts
- **InitContainer**: Provisions dependencies (jq, Python) before the main application starts
- **PVC mounting**: Shared storage for raw input and processed output paths
- **JSON sanitization**: ConfigMap-driven key stripping using jq/Python
- **Sidecar pattern**: Log shipping via tail + HTTP POST to an external service
- **Probes**: Readiness and liveness endpoints for Kubernetes scheduling decisions
- **preStop hook**: Ensures graceful shutdown before container termination
- **PodDisruptionBudget**: Maintains minimum availability during voluntary disruptions

questions.question: In CKAD terms, implement a per-namespace health-checker: a Deployment that reads HEALTH_ENDPOINT and INTERVAL from a ConfigMap, a token from a Secret, runs a loop hitting the endpoint every INTERVAL seconds, and POSTs a summary to a central endpoint; include InitContainer to install curl, a TLS sidecar for cert rotation, readiness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
Implement a per-namespace health-checker with a Deployment that reads `HEALTH_ENDPOINT` and `INTERVAL` from a ConfigMap, retrieves a token from a Secret, runs a loop hitting the endpoint every `INTERVAL` seconds, and POSTs a summary to a central endpoint.

## Minimal YAML Skeleton

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: health-checker-config
data:
  HEALTH_ENDPOINT: "http://service-a:8080/health"
  INTERVAL: "30"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: health-checker-secret
type: Opaque
stringData:
  API_TOKEN: "your-token-here"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: health-checker
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: health-checker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: health-checker
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-curl
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tools
          mountPath: /tools
      containers:
      - name: health-checker
        image: alpine:3.18
        env:
        - name: HEALTH_ENDPOINT
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: health-checker-config
              key: HEALTH_ENDPOINT
        - name: INTERVAL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: health-checker-config
              key: INTERVAL
        - name: API_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: health-checker-secret
              key: API_TOKEN
        command: ["sh", "-c"]
        args:
        - |
          while true; do
            RESPONSE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" $HEALTH_ENDPOINT)
            curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_TOKEN" \
              -d "{\"namespace\":\"$NAMESPACE\",\"status\":\"$RESPONSE\"}" \
              https://central-api/summary
            sleep $INTERVAL
          done
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          exec:
            command: ["cat", "/tmp/healthy"]
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      - name: tls-sidecar
        image: cert-rotator:latest
        securityContext:
          capabilities:
            add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]
      volumes:
      - name: tools
        emptyDir: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: health-checker-netpol
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: health-checker
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: internal-services
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector: {}
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

## Trade-offs

| Aspect | Trade-off |
|--------|-----------|
| **InitContainer vs. Custom Image** | InitContainer adds startup latency but keeps the main image lean. A custom image with curl baked in starts faster but results in a larger image. |
| **Alpine vs. BusyBox** | Alpine is smaller but uses musl libc, which may cause curl compatibility issues. BusyBox is more universal but has fewer built-in tools. |
| **TLS Sidecar** | Improves security but consumes additional resources and adds cert-rotation complexity. Consider Istio or Linkerd for managed mTLS instead. |
| **NetworkPolicy** | Restricting egress enhances security but may break functionality if endpoints change, and requires proper namespace labeling. |
| **Readiness Probe** | Prevents traffic to unhealthy pods but adds latency during pod startup. A misconfigured liveness probe can cause unnecessary restarts. |
| **Infinite Loop in Container** | Simple and requires no external tools, but doesn't handle signals gracefully. Consider using a lightweight init system like `tini`. |
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question evaluates practical Kubernetes Application Developer (CKAD) skills across multiple resource types and patterns in a realistic production scenario. It tests the ability to compose distributed system components while understanding security boundaries.

## Key Concepts Tested

1. **ConfigMap/Secret Integration**: Properly referencing ConfigMap values and Secret data as environment variables to configure the application.

2. **InitContainer Pattern**: Performing one-time setup tasks—like installing tools—before the main application container starts.

3. **Sidecar Containers**: Running auxiliary containers alongside the main application for cross-cutting concerns like TLS certificate management.

4. **Health Probes**: Configuring readiness and liveness probes to manage pod traffic and restart behavior.

5. **NetworkPolicy**: Restricting network egress to enforce least-privilege access between namespaces and services.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

- Forgetting to mount ConfigMap/Secret values into the container environment
- Not handling signal propagation in infinite loops (leading to graceful shutdown issues)
- Overly restrictive NetworkPolicies that block necessary egress routes
- Misconfiguring probe thresholds that cause premature restarts or delayed traffic routing

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests CKAD fundamentals for per-namespace automation, using CronJobs, ConfigMaps, Secrets, InitContainers, and PVC-backed logs in a realistic heartbeat scenario.

## Key Concepts

- CronJob scheduling and Job templates
- ConfigMap and Secret usage for config and creds
- InitContainer for one-time setup
- Probes for container health
- PersistentVolumeClaim for logs

## Code Example

```javascript
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: heartbeat-cron
  namespace: default
spec:
  schedule: "*/10 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: install-deps
            image: alpine:3.19
            command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl"]
          containers:
          - name: heartbeater
            image: alpine:3.19
            command: ["sh","-c","curl -sS -X POST -H \"Authorization: Bearer $API_TOKEN\" $TARGET_URL/heartbeat -d '{\"ts\": \"$(date +%s)\"}'"]
            envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: cron-config
            - secretRef:
                name: target-creds
            volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["sh","-c","test -f /logs/ready"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 80
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: heartbeat-logs
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you rotate the API token without recreating the CronJob?
- What failure modes and retries would you implement?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of namespace-scoped quotas and limits, basic RBAC implications, and how quotas affect scheduling.

## Key Concepts
- ResourceQuota
- LimitRange
- Namespace NetworkPolicy
- Scheduling under quotas

## Code Example
````yaml
# Namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: team-a
---
# ResourceQuota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: '4'
    limits.memory: '8Gi'
---
# LimitRange
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: team-limit
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: '500m'
      memory: '256Mi'
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: '100m'
      memory: '128Mi'
    max:
      cpu: '2'
      memory: '2Gi'
    min:
      cpu: '100m'
      memory: '128Mi'
    type: Container
---
# Deployment (tests quota)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: test-app
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: test-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: test-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:alpine
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: '250m'
            memory: '256Mi'
          limits:
            cpu: '500m'
            memory: '512Mi'
---
# NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-internal-egress
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 10.0.0.0/8
````

Use an InitContainer to install psql and place it in /tools; readiness/liveness probes; a Namespace NetworkPolicy to restrict egress to the Postgres endpoint; store last run timestamp in a namespace ConfigMap migration-status. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD mastery of multi-resource coordination, init containers, volumes, secrets, and network policies. It also validates per-namespace config management and safe log persistence.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob and Job orchestration across a namespace
- InitContainer to install client tooling and share /tools
- ConfigMap and Secret usage for migrations and DB credentials
- PVC-backed /logs for auditability
- NetworkPolicy restricting egress to Postgres
- Status tracking in a namespace ConfigMap

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-migrate
spec:
  schedule: "*/12 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          volumes:
          - name: logs
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: logs-pvc
          - name: migrations
            configMap:
              name: migrations-config
          - name: tools
            emptyDir: {}
          initContainers:
          - name: installer
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache postgresql-client && mkdir -p /tools && cp /usr/bin/psql /tools/psql"]
            volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /tools
          containers:
          - name: migrate
            image: migrate-base:latest
            command: ["/tools/psql","-h","$(DB_HOST)","-U","$(DB_USER)","-d","$(DB_NAME)","-f","/migrations/*.sql"]
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: db-credentials
            - configMapRef:
                name: db-config
            volumeMounts:
            - name: migrations
              mountPath: /migrations
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Trade-offs
- InitContainer size vs main image size; tooling duplication
- Per-namespace migrations risk drift vs centralized control
- Secret rotation complexity; ensure timely updates
- Logging volume retention vs cost

  Migrate -->|from /migrations| MigrationsConfig[ConfigMap: migrations-config]
  CronJob -->|12m| Job

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD-level control plane skills: per-namespace isolation, ConfigMap/Secret usage, InitContainer, PVC logging, readiness/liveness, and NetworkPolicy.

## Key Concepts
- Deployment, ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, InitContainer, probes, NetworkPolicy
- Watching resources with a small controller; security and scalability trade-offs

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: policy-enforcer
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: policy-enforcer
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
      containers:
      - name: enforcer
        image: registry/policy-enforcer:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: policy-config
        - secretRef:
            name: policy-auth
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: logs-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the policy without a live cluster?
- What are the implications of running a per-namespace controller at scale?

Provide a minimal manifest skeleton including InitContainer to install jq and curl, a ConfigMap for batchSize and endpoint, a Secret for API creds, a TLS sidecar for cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the enrichment endpoint and enriched storage. How would you validate under load and rollback?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD skills: multi-container pod design, InitContainers, PVCs, ConfigMaps, Secrets, TLS sidecars, probes, PDBs, and network policies, plus load validation and rollback strategies.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainer for tooling setup
- PVC mounting and data direction between /data/events and /data/enriched
- ConfigMap/Secret for runtime config and credentials
- TLS sidecar for certificate rotation
- Readiness and Liveness probes
- PodDisruptionBudget
- NetworkPolicy for scoped egress
- Load testing and rollback workflow

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: config-aggregator-config
data:
  batchSize: "100"
  enrichmentEndpoint: "http://enrich-controller.analytics.svc.cluster.local:8080/enrich"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: config-aggregator-creds
type: Opaque
data:
  apiToken: <base64-encoded-token>
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: config-aggregator
  namespace: analytics
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: config-aggregator
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: config-aggregator
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: init-tools
          image: alpine:3.17
          command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl jq; sleep 0"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /data
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: myrepo/config-aggregator:latest
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: config-aggregator-config
            - secretRef:
                name: config-aggregator-creds
          volumeMounts:
            - name: events
              mountPath: /data/events
            - name: enriched
              mountPath: /data/enriched
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 9000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 5
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /readyz
              port: 9000
            initialDelaySeconds: 20
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: events
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: config-aggregator-events-pvc
        - name: enriched
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: config-aggregator-enriched-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt this for namespace isolation across multiple environments?
- What scaling considerations arise with batchSize tuning and backpressure?
- How would you observe and debug under intermittent enrichment endpoint failures?

Use an InitContainer to install pigz. A script must read DAYS_OLDER from ConfigMap backup-config (default 7), tar.gz dirs under /data older than the threshold into /backups/backup-YYYY-MM-DD.tar.gz, then POST a summary to http://backup-portal:8080/api/v1/backup-summary. Include resources, backoffLimit=3, successfulJobsHistoryLimit=3, readiness and liveness probes, and a PodDisruptionBudget. Explain verification and rollback steps?
questions.answer: Create a CronJob data-archiver in nightly-backups with schedule 0 2 * * *. It mounts data-pvc:/data and backups-pvc:/backups. InitContainer installs pigz. A script reads DAYS_OLDER from ConfigMap back
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a scheduled, idempotent data archival workflow using core Kubernetes primitives (CronJob, PVCs, ConfigMap, InitContainer, HTTP post, probes) with practical considerations for reliability (backoff, history limits) and operations (PDB).

## Key Concepts
- CronJob scheduling and job lifecycles
- PVC data/input and output destinations
- InitContainer usage for tooling installation
- ConfigMap-driven thresholds and defaults
- Resource requests/limits, probes, backoff, and RBAC considerations
- PodDisruptionBudget applicability to ephemeral batch workloads

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton patch (CronJob)
apiVersion: batch/v1beta1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: data-archiver
  namespace: nightly-backups
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 3
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: archiver
            image: my-registry/archiver:latest
            volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /data
            - name: backups
              mountPath: /backups
            resources:
              requests:
                cpu: "100m"
                memory: "128Mi"
              limits:
                cpu: "500m"
                memory: "256Mi"
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "/usr/local/bin/archiver.sh"]
            readinessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "test -d /data && true"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 10
              periodSeconds: 30
            livenessProbe:
              exec:
                command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "test -f /backups/backup-*.tar.gz || true"]
              initialDelaySeconds: 30
              periodSeconds: 60
          volumes:
          - name: data
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: data-pvc
          - name: backups
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: backups-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
        
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test behavior when backup-config DAYs_OLDER changes in-flight?
- How would you rollback a failed run and protect against partial backups?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Source: /data PVC] --> B[Archival script runs nightly]
  B --> C[Writes to /backups PVC as backup-YYYY-MM-DD.tar.gz]
  C --> D[POST summary to backup-portal]
  D --> E[Pod completes; Job history retained]
