questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD fundamentals: per-namespace scope, ConfigMaps, Secrets, InitContainers, and NetworkPolicy, plus practical image-signing workflow.

## Key Concepts
- PVC-mounted manifests and per-namespace scope
- InitContainer install and bootstrapping binaries (cosign)
- Batch processing using BATCH_SIZE from ConfigMap
- Secret-based API credentials and TLS sidecar rotation
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- NetworkPolicy restricting egress to internal signing endpoint

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton of image-signer Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: image-signer
  namespace: <ns>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: image-signer
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: image-signer
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-tools
        image: appropriate/curl
        command: ["sh", "-lc", "apk add --no-cache cosign jq; true"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /manifests
      containers:
      - name: signer
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-lc", "cron -f"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: manifests
          mountPath: /manifests
      volumes:
      - name: manifests
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: manifest-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test with dry-run and kubeval? 
- How would you handle large namespaces with many manifests?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace metrics-forwarder: a Deployment tails /metrics.log from a PVC, aggregates metrics in 1-minute windows, and forwards to an internal Prometheus Pushgateway at prom-gateway:9091. Use a ConfigMap to set BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS, a Secret for credentials, an InitContainer to install curl and jq, and a sidecar for TLS cert rotation. Include readiness probes, resource requests, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the pushgateway. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
Design a per-namespace Deployment that tails /metrics.log from a PVC, aggregates metrics into 1-minute windows, and pushes to an internal Prometheus Pushgateway at prom-gateway:9091 over TLS. A ConfigMap configures the BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS parameter, a Secret stores credentials, an InitContainer installs curl and jq, and a sidecar handles TLS certificate rotation. The deployment includes readiness and liveness probes, resource requests and limits, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the pushgateway only.

```yaml
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: metrics-forwarder-config
  namespace: target-namespace
data:
  BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS: "60"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: metrics-forwarder-credentials
  namespace: target-namespace
type: Opaque
stringData:
  API_KEY: "your-api-key-here"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: metrics-forwarder
  namespace: target-namespace
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: metrics-forwarder
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: metrics-forwarder
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: metrics-forwarder
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: metrics-forwarder
      initContainers:
        - name: install-tools
          image: curlimages/curl:latest
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
          args:
            - apk add --no-cache jq bash
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: false
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 50m
              memory: 32Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 64Mi
      containers:
        - name: processor
          image: your-metrics-processor:latest
          env:
            - name: BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: metrics-forwarder-config
                  key: BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS
            - name: PUSHGATEWAY_URL
              value: "http://prom-gateway:9091"
            - name: API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: metrics-forwarder-credentials
                  key: API_KEY
          volumeMounts:
            - name: metrics-volume
              mountPath: /metrics
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 256Mi
        - name: cert-rotator
          image: cert-rotator-sidecar:latest
          securityContext:
            capabilities:
              add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 50m
              memory: 64Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
      volumes:
        - name: metrics-volume
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: metrics-log-pvc
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: metrics-forwarder-egress
  namespace: target-namespace
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: metrics-forwarder
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: monitoring
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: prometheus-pushgateway
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 9091
```

**Trade-offs:**

1. **InitContainer vs. multi-stage image:** An InitContainer adds startup latency but keeps the main container image lean. A multi-stage build bakes tools into the image, reducing pod startup time at the cost of a larger image.

2. **Sidecar vs. embedded TLS termination:** A dedicated cert-rotator sidecar separates concerns but increases resource usage and complexity. Embedding TLS logic in the main container simplifies deployment but couples certificate management to application logic.

3. **Pushgateway vs. Prometheus scraping:** Pushgateway suits batch/metric aggregation since the processor controls when data is sent. However, it creates a single point of failure and can become a bottleneck—consider redundant Pushgateway instances for production.

4. **ConfigMap for BATCH_WINDOW_SECONDS:** Provides runtime configurability without rebuilding, but updates require a pod restart unless using a volume-mount approach with automatic reloading.

5. **NetworkPolicy egress restriction:** Restricts the blast radius but requires careful label management and assumes the pushgateway namespace is predictable. Namespace label changes must be coordinated.

6. **Readiness vs. liveness probes:** Readiness gates traffic until the processor is caught up; liveness ensures the container restarts if stuck. Both are essential but require tuning for the 1-minute batch window to avoid premature failures."
questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question evaluates a candidate's ability to design production-ready, per-namespace observability data flows—a critical skill for platform engineers and SREs. It tests familiarity with Kubernetes security primitives, resource management, and operational patterns that generic deployments often overlook.

## Key Concepts Tested

- **Per-namespace isolation:** Scoping a Deployment to each namespace ensures data locality and prevents cross-namespace metric contamination.
- **PVC-backed data flow:** Tailing from a PVC provides durability and allows pods to restart without data loss.
- **Security hardening:** NetworkPolicies, Secrets, and resource limits demonstrate a defense-in-depth approach.
- **Operational concerns:** Probes, InitContainers, and sidecars reflect real-world operational complexity.

## Common Pitfalls

Candidates often miss NetworkPolicy egress rules, underconfigure resource limits, or overlook the trade-offs between Pushgateway reliability and simplicity. Strong candidates will proactively discuss alternatives like Prometheus scraping or distributed tracing backends.

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace image-pull-policy enforcer: a Deployment runs a controller in the target namespace that scans Deployments for imagePullPolicy != Always, patches to Always, and triggers a rolling update by annotating the Deployment; logs to a PVC /logs; InitContainer installs kubectl; ConfigMap drives behavior, Secret supplies webhook creds to POST a summary; include readiness/liveness probes and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress?
questions.answer: Design a per-namespace image-pull-policy enforcer: a Deployment runs a controller in the target namespace that scans Deployments for imagePullPolicy != Always, patches to Always, and triggers a rollin
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests the ability to implement namespace-scoped policy tooling, performing API reads/patches, triggering rollouts, and handling observability with PVC logs and external reporting.

## Key Concepts

- Kubernetes API: list and patch Deployments in a namespace
- imagePullPolicy Always and rolling updates via annotations
- InitContainer to prepare kubectl
- PVC-backed logs collection
- ConfigMap and Secret for runtime config and webhook credentials
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- Namespace NetworkPolicy for restricted egress

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: image-pull-policy-enforcer
  namespace: <target-namespace>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: image-pull-policy-enforcer
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: image-pull-policy-enforcer
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-kubectl
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl kubectl"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: enforcer
        image: myorg/enforcer:latest
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: enforcer-config
        - secretRef:
            name: enforcer-webhook-creds
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: enforcer-logs
      restartPolicy: Always
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test idempotency of patches in CI/CD?
- What are failure modes if the webhook is unreachable?

questions.question: In namespace analytics, implement a CronJob named data-pruner that hourly creates a Job to prune /data/dumps in a PVC. PRUNE_DAYS comes from prune-config ConfigMap. The Job uses Alpine, with an InitContainer to stage /scripts/prune.sh from the ConfigMap. Include TTLAfterFinished: 86400, readinessProbe and livenessProbe on /healthz, and resource requests/limits. Use a minimal RBAC ServiceAccount; a Namespace NetworkPolicy allowing egress only to internal-api.analytics.svc.cluster.local:443. Verify under load by aging files and rollback by deleting the CronJob?
questions.answer: Configure a CronJob data-pruner that hourly creates a Job to prune /data/dumps in a PVC. PRUNE_DAYS comes from prune-config ConfigMap. The Job uses Alpine with InitContainer to stage /scripts/prune.sh
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses practical CKAD skills: CronJob/Job TTL, InitContainers, ConfigMaps, PVCs, probes, RBAC, and NetworkPolicy with a realistic data-pruning task.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob and TTLAfterFinished
- InitContainer to stage scripts
- ConfigMap-driven parameters
- PVC mounting
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- RBAC least privilege via ServiceAccount
- NetworkPolicy egress control

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: data-pruner
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: analytics-pruner
          initContainers:
          - name: stage-script
            image: busybox
            command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /scripts/prune.sh /prune.sh"]
          containers:
          - name: prune
            image: alpine:3.18
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "/prune.sh"]
            resources:
              requests:
                memory: "64Mi"
                cpu: "100m"
              limits:
                memory: "128Mi"
                cpu: "250m"
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 5
              periodSeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /healthz
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 15
              periodSeconds: 20
          volumes:
          - name: data
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: dumps-pvc
          - name: scripts
            configMap:
              name: prune-scripts
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test TTLAfterFinished behavior in a staging cluster?
- What monitoring would you add for prune activity and failure retry logic?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace cross-config-syncer: a Deployment in each app namespace mirrors shared-config from the config-plane namespace into a local app-config ConfigMap. A watcher loop polls every 30s via the Kubernetes API to apply deltas, with a ClusterRoleBinding granting access to ConfigMaps in the source namespace. Logs go to a PVC at /logs; a CronJob posts a heartbeat to central endpoint. Include minimal YAML skeletons and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Implement a per-namespace cross-config-syncer: a Deployment in each app namespace mirrors shared-config from the config-plane namespace into a local app-config ConfigMap. A watcher loop polls every 30
questions.explanation:
Why this is asked: tests RBAC across namespaces, watch-loop logic, ConfigMap synchronization, and logging. Candidates must outline: RBAC roles, cross-namespace API access, delta application, and failure handling. Key points:

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace GitOps runner: a Deployment in each namespace watches a ConfigMap named gitops-config containing repo URL and a target path; an InitContainer installs git and tooling, then clones the repo into an emptyDir, runs kubeval and yamllint, and applies manifests with kubectl apply -f. Status is written to a per-namespace ConfigMap gitops-status and logs go to a PVC at /logs. Include a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Propose a per-namespace GitOps runner: a Deployment with one Pod; an InitContainer installs git/yq, clones the repo from the ConfigMap-provided URL into /workspace, runs kubeval/yamllint, then kubectl apply.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical CKAD ability to implement per-namespace GitOps, use InitContainers, ConfigMaps, PVCs, and NetworkPolicy; validates YAML tooling, apply flow, and status reporting.

## Key Concepts
- Per-namespace GitOps design patterns
- InitContainer provisioning and tool installation
- YAML validation with kubeval and yamllint
- ConfigMap and Secret usage for configuration
- PVC-based log streaming and persistence
- Namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy for egress control
- Drift detection and status reporting via ConfigMap

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton for a per-namespace GitOps runner
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: gitops-runner
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: gitops-runner
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: gitops-runner
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: git-clone
        image: alpine/git:latest
        env:
        - name: REPO_URL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: gitops-config
              key: repo-url
        - name: TARGET_PATH
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: gitops-config
              key: target-path
        volumeMounts:
        - name: workspace
          mountPath: /workspace
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - |
          apk add --no-cache git yq
          git clone $(REPO_URL) /workspace/repo
          cd /workspace/repo
          yq eval '.spec.path = "$(TARGET_PATH)"' . > manifests.yaml
      containers:
      - name: validator
        image: kubeval/kubeval:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: workspace
          mountPath: /workspace
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - |
          kubeval /workspace/manifests.yaml > /logs/validation.log 2>&1
          yamllint -d relaxed /workspace/manifests.yaml >> /logs/validation.log 2>&1
          kubectl apply -f /workspace/manifests.yaml --namespace=$(NAMESPACE)
          kubectl get -f /workspace/manifests.yaml -o jsonpath='{.status}' > /gitops-status
      volumes:
      - name: workspace
        emptyDir: {}
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: gitops-logs
```
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  ConfigMapGitOps[gitops-config] --> Init[InitContainer clones repo]
  Init --> Validator[yamllint/kubeval]
  Validator --> Apply[kubectl apply]
  Apply --> Status[gitops-status ConfigMap]
  Apply --> Logs[PersistentVolumeClaim /logs]

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace CronJob-based worker that watches a PVC-backed /data directory for new CSV files, processes them in batches (configurable via a ConfigMap named app-config with BATCH_SIZE), converts to JSON, and POSTS to a cluster-internal endpoint at http://data-portal:8080/api/v1/ingest. Use an InitContainer to install csvkit, a Secret for API credentials, a Sidecar for TLS cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, a Namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint, and a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Deploy a per-namespace CronJob that spawns a Job every 5 minutes to read CSVs from a PVC at /data, batch-process up to BATCH_SIZE files, convert to JSON, and POST to http://data-portal:8080/api/v1/ingest.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD workflow patterns: CronJobs, batch processing, and secret management within a namespace; validates knowledge of init containers, sidecars, and network hardening.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob scheduling in a namespace
- InitContainer dependency installation
- ConfigMap for BATCH_SIZE
- Secret for API credentials
- Sidecar for TLS cert rotation
- NetworkPolicy for internal egress controls
- Probes for readiness and liveness
- Idempotency vs at-least-once semantics

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: csv-ingest
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          initContainers:
          - name: install-csvkit
            image: python:3.9-alpine
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'pip install csvkit && cp -r /usr/local/lib/python3.9/site-packages/* /shared/']
            volumeMounts:
            - name: shared-libs
              mountPath: /shared
          containers:
          - name: csv-processor
            image: python:3.9-alpine
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'python /app/process.py']
            env:
            - name: BATCH_SIZE
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: BATCH_SIZE
            - name: API_TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: api-credentials
                  key: token
            volumeMounts:
            - name: data-pvc
              mountPath: /data
            - name: shared-libs
              mountPath: /usr/local/lib/python3.9/site-packages
            readinessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /health
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 10
            livenessProbe:
              httpGet:
                path: /health
                port: 8080
              initialDelaySeconds: 30
          - name: cert-rotator
            image: cert-manager/cert-manager-rotator
            volumeMounts:
            - name: certs
              mountPath: /etc/ssl/certs
          volumes:
          - name: data-pvc
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: data-pvc
          - name: shared-libs
            emptyDir: {}
          - name: certs
            emptyDir: {}
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  BATCH_SIZE: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-credentials
type: Opaque
data:
  token: <base64-encoded-token>
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: csv-ingest-netpol
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: csv-ingest
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: data-portal
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
```

## Trade-offs
- **CronJob vs Deployment**: CronJobs provide scheduled execution but lack continuous availability; Deployments offer always-on processing but require custom scheduling logic.
- **InitContainer overhead**: Installing dependencies at runtime increases startup time vs pre-built images, but provides flexibility for version updates.
- **Sidecar complexity**: TLS cert rotation adds operational overhead but ensures security compliance; alternative is using cert-manager injection.
- **Batch processing**: Larger batches improve throughput but increase memory usage and failure impact; smaller batches provide better isolation but higher API call frequency.
- **NetworkPolicy scope**: Namespace-scoped policies provide good isolation but require per-namespace management; cluster-wide policies simplify administration but reduce granular control.
- **Storage patterns**: PVC-backed storage ensures data persistence across pod restarts but requires careful volume management; temporary storage simplifies cleanup but risks data loss during failures.
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[CronJob] --> B[Job] --> C[//data CSVs/]
  B --> D[Transform to JSON]
  D --> E[POST to data-portal]
  E --> F[Success]
  F --> G[Cleanup / data]
  G --> H[Observability: metrics & logs]

questions.question: Design a per-namespace CKAD microservice that exposes a REST endpoint and supports dynamic feature flags controlled by a ConfigMap. The ConfigMap contains JSON features; a Secret stores per-namespace API keys. Include an InitContainer to install runtime dependencies, a sidecar that watches the ConfigMap for changes and signals the main container to reload config, readiness/liveness probes, an HPA based on 95th percentile latency, and a PVC-backed log store. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Deploy a per-namespace Deployment with an InitContainer that installs runtime deps; main app reads features.json from a ConfigMap and API keys from a Secret; a sidecar watches the ConfigMap for change
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to integrate ConfigMap-driven feature flags, per-namespace isolation, hot-reload semantics, and production considerations like latency-based scaling and persistent logs.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap-driven feature flags with JSON payloads
- Secret usage for per-namespace API keys
- InitContainer for dependencies
- ConfigMap reload sidecar pattern (hot reload via signals)
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- HPA based on latency
- PVC-backed logs

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ns-microservice
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ns-microservice
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ns-microservice
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: deps
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq && mkdir -p /logs"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-org/ns-microservice:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: ns-api-keys
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      - name: config-reload
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep infinity"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /config
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: ns-logs-pvc
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: features-config
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you implement a graceful failover if the ConfigMap reload signal fails to reach the main app?
- What are the trade-offs of hot-reload versus full pod restart for feature flag changes?

questions.answer: Patch the NetworkPolicy to add an egress rule for TCP 443 to api.analytics.internal from pods labeled app=reporter in namespace analytics, keeping existing rules. Apply, then verify with a test pod cu
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical NetworkPolicy understanding, especially egress limitations and debugging under load.

## Key Concepts

- NetworkPolicy
- Egress rules
- IPBlock vs DNS constraints
- Load validation
- Rollback strategy

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: reporter-egress
  namespace: analytics
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: reporter
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 10.2.0.0/16
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

## Follow-up Questions

- What risks exist with overly permissive egress policies, and how would you mitigate them?

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace Deployment that runs a small REST API (example Flask app) with config from a ConfigMap named app-config and an API key from Secret named api-credentials. Include an InitContainer to install dependencies, a sidecar to rotate TLS certs from a Secret named tls-certs, readiness/liveness probes, a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to DEST_ENDPOINT, and a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Outline: a Deployment with 1) ConfigMap app-config providing DEST_ENDPOINT and LOG_LEVEL as env vars, 2) Secret api-credentials for API_KEY, 3) InitContainer to install Python deps, 4) main REST API c
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to compose CKAD tasks involving ConfigMaps, Secrets, InitContainers, sidecars, network policies, and probes in a beginner-credible scenario.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainer for bootstrapping deps
- Sidecar for TLS cert rotation
- ConfigMap/Secret usage for config and credentials
- Namespace NetworkPolicy for egress control
- Readiness/Liveness probes and non-root/container security

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal skeleton highlights (not full manifest)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-deploy
  namespace: payments
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: installer
          image: python:3.9-slim
          command: ["sh", "-c", "pip install flask"]
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: my-registry/api:latest
          env:
            - name: DEST_ENDPOINT
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: DEST_ENDPOINT
            - name: LOG_LEVEL
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: LOG_LEVEL
            - name: API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: api-credentials
                  key: API_KEY
      volumes:
        - name: tls
          secret:
            secretName: tls-certs
      # probes, securityContext, etc.
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the NetworkPolicy in CI? 
- What are the risks of cert rotation on service availability?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Deployment] --> B[InitContainer: install deps]
  A --> C[Main container: REST API]
  A --> D[Sidecar: TLS rotation]
  B --> E[ConfigMap: app-config]
  B --> F[Secret: api-credentials]
  A --> G[NetworkPolicy: allow DEST_ENDPOINT]

questions.question: Design a per-namespace log-forwarder in CKAD terms: a Deployment that tails logs from a PVC-mounted /logs directory produced by apps in the namespace, aggregates log counts by severity for the last minute, and POSTs a heartbeat payload plus the count to a REST endpoint defined in a per-namespace ConfigMap ENDPOINT using API_TOKEN from a Secret. On failure, write payloads to a DLQ directory in the same PVC. Include an InitContainer to install curl and jq, readiness/liveness probes, and a minimal YAML skeleton; discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Deploy a per-namespace log-forwarder: a single-rep Deployment that mounts a PVC at /logs and /logs/dlq. InitContainer installs curl/jq. The forwarder tails new lines in /logs, classifies by severity,
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD patterns for per-namespace resources, log pipelines, and robust error handling with limited tooling.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped Deployments and PVCs
- InitContainers for bootstrap tooling
- ConfigMap/Secret for endpoint and credentials
- Simple in-Pod state via streaming tail and per-minute aggregation
- DLQ on PVC for failed forwards
- Readiness/Liveness probes

## Code Example

```yaml
# skeleton Deployment (high level)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: log-forwarder
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: log-forwarder
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: installer
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl jq"]
      containers:
        - name: forwarder
          image: some-forwarder:latest
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
            - name: dlq
              mountPath: /logs/dlq
          env:
            - name: ENDPOINT
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: log-forwarder-config
                  key: ENDPOINT
            - name: API_TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: log-forwarder-secret
                  key: API_TOKEN
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: logs-pvc
        - name: dlq
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: logs-dlq-pvc
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale and parallelize across namespaces?
- How to secure endpoint access and rotate API_TOKEN?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  N[Namespace PVC: /logs] --> I[InitContainer: install curl/jq]
  I --> F[Forwarder: tail /logs, aggregate last-minute counts by severity]
  F --> E[POST to ENDPOINT with TOKEN from Secret]
  E -->|success| S[Healthy]
  E -->|failure| DLQ[DLQ: /logs/dlq]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked Tests ability to compose common CKAD primitives: Deployment, InitContainer writing into a PVC, ConfigMap/Secret handling, basic auth, and network controls at namespace scope. ## Key Concepts - InitContainer writes dynamic index.html from ConfigMap data - PVC for content, ConfigMap for config, Secret for credentials - Readiness/Liveness probes, TLS/basics via htpasswd - Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting external access ## Code Example ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: site namespace: ns-example spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: site template: metadata: labels: app: site spec: initContainers: - name: gen-index image: alpine:3.18 command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "SITE_TITLE=$(cat /config/SITE_TITLE); FOOTER=$(cat /config/FOOTER); cat > /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html <$SITE_TITLE

$SITE_TITLE

$FOOTER

\nEOF"] volumeMounts: - name: html mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html - name: config mountPath: /config containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.25-alpine ports: - containerPort: 80 volumeMounts: - name: html mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html readinessProbe: httpGet: path: / port: 80 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 10 livenessProbe: httpGet: path: / port: 80 initialDelaySeconds: 15 periodSeconds: 20 volumes: - name: html persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: site-content - name: config configMap: name: site-config - name: auth secret: secretName: site-auth ``` ## Follow-up Questions - How would you rotate the htpasswd file without downtime? - How would you test the readiness probe in CI/CD?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD ability to combine common primitives: PVC-backed data, ConfigMap-driven behavior, Secrets for credentials, InitContainers, TLS rotation sidecar, probes, and per-namespace NetworkPolicy in a cohesive, beginner-friendly scenario.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap/Secret usage for runtime config and credentials
- PVC-backed storage and data lifecycle

- Readiness/Liveness probes and health checks
- NamespaceNetworkPolicy scoping

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ns-api
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ns-api
    spec:
      volumes:
        - name: data
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: ns-app-data
      initContainers:
        - name: install-deps
          image: python:3.11
          command: ["bash","-lc","pip install flask sqlite3"]
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: myregistry/ns-api:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: ENABLE_FEATURE
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: ENABLE_FEATURE
            - name: API_TIMEOUT
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: API_TIMEOUT
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /data
      - name: tls-sidecar
        image: quay.io/cert_manager/tls-sidecar:latest
        # sidecar config omitted for brevity
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      restartPolicy: Always
```

- What are trade-offs of storing DB in a PVC vs ephemeral storage?

Include an InitContainer to install jq and a validator, a Sidecar for TLS cert rotation, readiness/liveness probes, a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint, and a minimal YAML skeleton. Discuss trade-offs?

Include an InitContainer to install jq and validation tools, a sidecar for TLS certificate rotation, proper readiness/liveness probes, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy restricting egress to the internal endpoint.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
CKAD candidates often struggle with end-to-end file ingestion using Kubernetes primitives. This question tests volume access, ConfigMap/Secret usage, InitContainers, probes, NetworkPolicy, and retry strategies in a comprehensive scenario.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMaps/Secrets for configuration and credentials
- InitContainers for tooling setup
- In-cluster networking and security policies
- File-based ingestion with idempotent POST operations
- Multi-container pod patterns

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo-implementation sketch
const payload = readFile('/input/file.json');
validateSchema(payload, getSchema('schema-config'));
const normalized = normalize(payload);
sendToEndpoint('http://data-portal:8080/api/v1/ingest', normalized);
```

## Trade-offs
- **File watching vs. event-driven**: File watching is simpler but less efficient than event-based systems
- **In-cluster validation**: Validates locally but increases pod resource usage
- **NetworkPolicy restrictions**: Improves security but adds complexity to egress management
- **Sidecar overhead**: TLS rotation adds reliability but increases resource consumption

questions.question: In namespace data, create a CronJob named daily-analytics that runs at 02:00 daily using image analytics-runner:2.0. It must mount a ConfigMap analytics-config (DB_HOST, DB_NAME, QUERY_TIMEOUT) and a Secret analytics-creds (DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD). Enforce no overlaps (concurrencyPolicy: Forbid), backoffLimit: 3, TTLSecondsAfterFinished: 86400, successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3, and annotate the PodTemplate with a checksum/config to reload on changes. How would you verify this under simulated load and when the config changes?
questions.answer:
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: daily-analytics
  namespace: data
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  backoffLimit: 3
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            checksum/config: "$(cat analytics-config.yaml | sha256sum)"
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: analytics
            image: analytics-runner:2.0
            envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: analytics-config
            - secretRef:
                name: analytics-creds
```
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design production-ready batch workloads in Kubernetes, including secure configuration management, job lifecycle controls, and deployment strategies for config-driven restarts.

## Key Concepts
- **concurrencyPolicy: Forbid** - Prevents overlapping job executions, ensuring data consistency
- **backoffLimit: 3** - Limits retry attempts for failed pods before marking the job as failed
- **ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400** - Auto-cleansup completed jobs after 24 hours
- **successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3** - Retains recent successful job history for debugging/auditing
- **checksum annotation** - Triggers pod recreation when ConfigMap/Secret changes (requires script to generate hash)
- **envFrom** - Mounts all ConfigMap/Secret values as environment variables without explicit key mapping

## Verification Under Simulated Load
1. **Test concurrency**: Trigger manual job while another runs - should fail with "Cannot determine if job needs to be started"
2. **Test failure handling**: Set invalid config, verify backoff retries 3 times
3. **Test cleanup**: Check completed jobs are removed after 24h TTL

## Verification On Config Change
1. Update ConfigMap: `kubectl apply -f analytics-config.yaml`
2. Verify annotation checksum updates in next job
3. Confirm new pod picks up new environment variables
4. Use `kubectl get pods` to verify new pod creation timestamp

## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical use of per-namespace isolation for log processing, dynamic config via ConfigMap, secret-based credentials, and a robust data path with batching, retries, and a DLQ. Requires InitContainer setup, sidecars for TLS, and proper networking constraints.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap/Secret usage for runtime config and sensitive data
- InitContainer for dependencies
- Sidecar for TLS cert rotation
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- NetworkPolicy scoping to internal endpoint
- DLQ on PVC for failed payloads

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: log-collector
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: log-collector
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: init-deps
          image: python:3.11-slim
          command: ["sh", "-c", "pip install --no-cache-dir requests"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
      containers:
        - name: collector
          image: my org/log-collector:latest
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
          env:
            - name: BATCH_SIZE
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: app-config
                  key: BATCH_SIZE
        - name: tls-sidecar
          image: myorg/tls-sidecar:latest
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tls
              mountPath: /tls
      volumes:
        - name: logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: logs-pvc
        - name: tls
          secret:
            secretName: tls-certs
        - name: dlq
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: log-dlq-pvc
      # NetworkPolicy defined in separate manifest
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle backpressure if analytics.local is slow to ingest?
- What changes would you make for multi-tenant isolation across namespaces?

questions.question: In a CKAD scenario, in namespace dataops, create a CronJob named db-backup that runs daily at 02:00 UTC to back up a PostgreSQL database. Use a ConfigMap backup-config for BACKUP_DIR and DB_HOST and a Secret db-credentials for DB_USER and DB_PASSWORD. Mount PVC backup-pvc at /backups and write backups as /backups/<dbname>-YYYYMMDD.sql. Enforce concurrencyPolicy: Forbid, keep 7 successful jobs, and set activeDeadlineSeconds: 7200. Provide manifests and a straightforward validation and rollback plan?
questions.answer: Create a CronJob manifest and related resources to back up PostgreSQL daily. Use ConfigMap backup-config for BACKUP_DIR and DB_HOST, Secret db-credentials for DB_USER/DB_PASSWORD, mount PVC backup-pvc at /backups, and write backups as /backups/<dbname>-YYYYMMDD.sql. Enforce concurrencyPolicy: Forbid, keep 7 successful jobs, and set activeDeadlineSeconds: 7200.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to compose Kubernetes primitives for practical automation: CronJobs, Jobs, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and PVCs, plus robust scheduling and rollback.

## Key Concepts
- CronJob scheduling and jobTemplate usage
- ConfigMap and Secret as environment sources
- PVC mounting in Jobs for persistent backups
- Concurrency policy and job history controls
- Rollback and validation workflows

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-backup
  namespace: dataops
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 7
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 7200
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: postgres-backup
            image: postgres:15
            env:
            - name: BACKUP_DIR
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: backup-config
                  key: BACKUP_DIR
            - name: DB_HOST
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: backup-config
                  key: DB_HOST
            - name: DB_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-credentials
                  key: DB_USER
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: db-credentials
                  key: DB_PASSWORD
            command:
            - /bin/bash
            - -c
            - |
              DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)
              BACKUP_FILE="/backups/${DB_NAME}-${DATE}.sql"
              pg_dump -h $DB_HOST -U $DB_USER -d $DB_NAME > $BACKUP_FILE
            volumeMounts:
            - name: backup-storage
              mountPath: /backups
          volumes:
          - name: backup-storage
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: backup-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
```

## Validation Plan
1. Check CronJob creation: `kubectl get cronjob db-backup -n dataops`
2. Verify schedule: `kubectl describe cronjob db-backup -n dataops`
3. Test manual job: `kubectl create job --from=cronjob/db-backup test-backup -n dataops`
4. Monitor job completion: `kubectl get jobs -n dataops -w`
5. Validate backup files: `kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n dataops -- ls -la /backups`

## Rollback Plan
1. Delete CronJob: `kubectl delete cronjob db-backup -n dataops`
2. Clean up test jobs: `kubectl delete jobs -l job-name=db-backup -n dataops`
3. Remove backup files if needed: Access PVC and delete specific backup files
4. Recreate with corrected manifest if issues persist

questions.question: In CKAD terms, implement a per-namespace BackupPlan pattern (CRD) that triggers backups of a StatefulSet's PVC to a per-namespace S3-like bucket. Define CRD fields for targetStatefulSet, sourcePVC, destBucket, destPrefix; show a minimal Job-based workflow with an InitContainer to install awscli, a Secret for AWS creds, a ConfigMap for BUCKET/PREFIX, and a PVC for logs. Include readiness/liveness probes and a minimal YAML skeleton; discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer:
Implement a per-namespace BackupPlan CRD with fields for targetStatefulSet, sourcePVC, destBucket, and destPrefix. A lightweight controller watches BackupPlan resources in each namespace and creates a Job named backup-{plan-name} that performs the backup operation.

```yaml
# CRD Definition
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backupplans.example.com
spec:
  group: example.com
  versions:
    - name: v1
      served: true
      storage: true
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            spec:
              type: object
              properties:
                targetStatefulSet:
                  type: string
                sourcePVC:
                  type: string
                destBucket:
                  type: string
                destPrefix:
                  type: string
              required: [targetStatefulSet, sourcePVC, destBucket]
  scope: Namespaced

# BackupPlan Instance
apiVersion: example.com/v1
kind: BackupPlan
metadata:
  name: webapp-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  targetStatefulSet: webapp
  sourcePVC: webapp-data
  destBucket: prod-backups
  destPrefix: webapp/$(date +%Y%m%d)

# AWS Credentials Secret
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: aws-creds
  namespace: production
type: Opaque
data:
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: <base64-encoded>
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: <base64-encoded>

# Configuration ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: backup-config
  namespace: production
data:
  BUCKET: prod-backups
  PREFIX: webapp/$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Backup Job Template
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: backup-webapp-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: install-awscli
          image: alpine:3.18
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              apk add --no-cache py3-pip &&
              pip3 install awscli
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /usr/local/bin
      containers:
        - name: backup
          image: alpine:3.18
          command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              apk add --no-cache tar &&
              tar czf /backup/webapp-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).tar.gz /data/* &&
              aws s3 cp /backup/webapp-*.tar.gz s3://$BUCKET/$PREFIX/
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: aws-creds
            - configMapRef:
                name: backup-config
          volumeMounts:
            - name: source-data
              mountPath: /data
              readOnly: true
            - name: backup-storage
              mountPath: /backup
            - name: tools
              mountPath: /usr/local/bin
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - sh
                - -c
                - test -f /backup/webapp-*.tar.gz
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 5
          livenessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - sh
                - -c
                - pgrep -f aws
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: source-data
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: webapp-data
        - name: backup-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: backup-logs
        - name: tools
          emptyDir: {}
      restartPolicy: OnFailure

# PVC for Backup Logs
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: backup-logs
  namespace: production
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
```
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design Kubernetes-native backup patterns using CRD + Job orchestration, with proper use of InitContainers, Secrets, ConfigMaps, and PVs. Encourages thinking about per-namespace scoping, fault-tolerance, and minimal operator logic.

## Key Concepts
- CustomResourceDefinition and a namespace-scoped controller
- Jobs, InitContainers, and data packaging (tarball) for backups
- Secrets for credentials and ConfigMaps for configuration
- PVC usage for temporary/log data and readiness/liveness probes
- Idempotency, retries, and failure semantics

## Trade-offs Discussion

**Advantages:**
- Namespace isolation prevents cross-namespace interference
- Job-based approach provides built-in retry and failure handling
- InitContainers ensure dependencies are met before main workload
- Readiness/liveness probes provide operational visibility
- Secret/ConfigMap separation follows security best practices

**Limitations:**
- Requires custom controller for CRD watching and Job creation
- No incremental backup support (full tarball each time)
- AWS credentials stored in cluster (consider IRSA/EKS)
- No backup verification or restore automation
- PVC mounting requires proper permissions and access

**Alternatives:**
- Use Velero for comprehensive backup/restore solution
- Implement CSI snapshot for instant PVC backups
- Consider sidecar pattern for continuous backup
- Use CronJob instead of one-time Jobs for scheduled backups
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[BackupPlan Created in Namespace] --> B[Controller detects Plan]
  B --> C[Create Job backup-PLAN]
  C --> D[Job InitContainer installs awscli]
  D --> E[Main Container tarballs PVC data]
  E --> F[Uploads to s3://bucket/prefix/plan.tar.gz]
  F --> G[Status update in CRD / heartbeat ConfigMap]

questions.question: Design a per-namespace synthetic-traffic generator Deployment in CKAD terms: it reads TARGET_URL, PAYLOAD, and HEADERS from a Namespace ConfigMap, and a TOKEN from a Secret; an InitContainer installs curl. It should generate requests at a configurable rate, log each attempt to a PVC at /logs, and on failure move the payload to a DLQ on the same PVC. Include readiness/liveness probes and a CPU-based HPA; provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Use a Deployment with envFrom ConfigMap for TARGET_URL, PAYLOAD, HEADERS and a Secret TOKEN. An InitContainer installs curl (apk add --no-cache curl). A worker loop hits TARGET_URL at a configurable r
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests CKAD proficiency in namespace scoping, ConfigMap/Secret wiring, InitContainer usage, persistent logging, DLQ handling, and monitoring hooks.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped ConfigMaps and Secrets
- InitContainer to install curl
- Deployment with a worker loop
- PVC-backed logs and DLQ
- Readiness/Liveness probes
- HorizontalPodAutoscaler by CPU

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: traffic-gen
  namespace: example-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: traffic-gen
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: traffic-gen
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-curl
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache curl && mkdir -p /logs/dlq"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: curlimages/curl:8.1.2
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: traffic-config
        - secretRef:
            name: traffic-secret
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: traffic-logs-pvc
```
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  NS[Namespace] --> D[Deployment: traffic-gen]
  D --> IC[InitContainer: install curl]
  D --> W[Worker: traffic generator]
  W --> Logs[/logs]
  W --> DLQ[/logs/dlq]
  W --> Probes[Probes]
  W --> HPA[HPA: CPU]

questions.question: CKAD intermediate: In namespace prod, the 4-replica Deployment 'payments-api' must talk to an upstream processor at payments.proc.svc:443 using mTLS. Create minimal Kubernetes manifests to mount a Secret tls-secret containing client cert/key and CA, configure the container to use those certs, and restrict egress to only that endpoint via a NetworkPolicy. Show the patches and explain how you'd verify TLS and cert rotation?
questions.answer: Mount tls-secret at /certs in payments-api, set TLS_PATH=/certs, and configure the client to present the certificate when connecting to payments.proc.svc:443; add a NetworkPolicy that restricts egress
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests secret handling, TLS/mTLS basics, mounting certs, and correct network isolation for a real-world integration.

## Key Concepts
- Secrets and volume mounts
- TLS/Certificates and mTLS basics
- NetworkPolicy egress controls
- Rollout and secret rotation strategies

## Code Example
```javascript
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: tls-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  tls.crt: <base64>
  tls.key: <base64>
  ca.crt: <base64>
```

```javascript
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments-api
  namespace: prod
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: payments
        image: payments-api:latest
        env:
        - name: TLS_PATH
          value: /certs
        volumeMounts:
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /certs
      volumes:
      - name: tls
        secret:
          secretName: tls-secret
```

```javascript
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: payments-egress
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payments-api
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 203.0.113.45/32
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
```

> Note: DNS-based egress restrictions are not widely supported in NetworkPolicy; use the processor’s IP in the ipBlock.

questions.question: In CKAD terms, design a per-namespace Job that migrates data from a PVC /data/migrate by reading CSVs with csvkit (InitContainer installs tools), sending batched JSON to http://data-portal:8080/api/v1/migrate. Configure MIG_BATCH_SIZE in mig-config ConfigMap and API token in mig-creds Secret. Use a PVC-stored marker for idempotence, set backoffLimit, and add readiness/liveness probes. Provide a minimal YAML skeleton and discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Draft a per-namespace Job that migrates data from a PVC /data/migrate by reading CSVs with csvkit (InitContainer installs tools), sending batched JSON to http://data-portal:8080/api/v1/migrate. MIG_BA
questions.explanation:
Why This Is Asked
- Validates CKAD basics: Jobs, InitContainers, ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, probes in a practical data-migrate flow.
- Introduces idempotence with a marker, a common real-world concern.

Key Concepts
- Job lifecycle and completion guarantees
- InitContainer usage for tooling installation
- Runtime config from ConfigMaps and Secrets
- PVC-based idempotence markers and backoff settings
- Probes and minimal YAML scaffolding

Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal Job skeleton (pseudo)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-migrate
spec:
  backoffLimit: 3
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: csvkit-install
        image: alpine:latest
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache python3 py3-pip && pip3 install csvkit"]
      containers:
      - name: migrator
        image: curlimages/curl:7.88.0
        command: ["sh", "-c", "python3 migrate.py"]
        env:
        - name: MIG_BATCH_SIZE
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: mig-config
              key: MIG_BATCH_SIZE
        - name: API_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: mig-creds
              key: api-token
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data/migrate
        - name: marker
          mountPath: /data/marker
      volumes:
      - name: data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: migrate-pvc
      - name: marker
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: migrate-marker-pvc
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      # probes would be defined on the migrator container
```

Follow-up Questions
- How would you test idempotence across retries?
- How would you handle partial CSV failures and ensure exactly-once semantics?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Start] --> B[InitContainer: install csvkit]
  B --> C[Read /data/migrate]
  C --> D[Batch to /api/v1/migrate]
  D --> E[Write marker to PVC]
  E --> F[Job completes]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of combining Kubernetes primitives for a practical, beginner-to-intermediate task: ConfigMaps and Secrets for config and credentials, InitContainers for bootstrapping, a TLS rotation sidecar for security, probes for reliability, and a Namespace NetworkPolicy to constrain egress.

## Key Concepts
- ConfigMap and Secret usage in a single pod
- InitContainer for bootstrapping dependencies
- Sidecar for TLS cert rotation
- Readiness and Liveness probes
- Namespace NetworkPolicy to constrain egress

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: proxy
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: install-deps
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["/bin/sh","-lc","apk add --no-cache python3 py3-pip && pip3 install fastapi uvicorn httpx"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /config
      containers:
      - name: proxy
        image: myorg/proxy:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config
        - secretRef:
            name: api-credentials
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: app-config
      - name: certs
        secret:
          secretName: tls-certs
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the rate limiter under burst traffic?
- How would you rotate keys without downtime?

questions.question: CKAD intermediate: In namespace 'prod', a Deployment named 'image-processor' with 4 replicas experiences spikes under load. Propose and implement a minimal patch to: (a) add resource requests/limits and health probes; (b) configure an HPA targeting 75% CPU (min 3, max 10 replicas); (c) add a PodDisruptionBudget to keep at least 3 healthy pods; (d) tune rolling updates (maxUnavailable:1). Then describe how you'd validate under simulated load and rollback if needed?
questions.answer: Implement a comprehensive patch to the Deployment by adding resource requests/limits (cpu: 100m/500m, memory: 128Mi/512Mi), configuring readinessProbe and livenessProbe with /healthz endpoint, deploying an HPA with minReplicas: 3, maxReplicas: 10, targetCPUUtilizationPercent: 75, creating a PodDisruptionBudget with minAvailable: 3, and setting rollingUpdate strategy with maxUnavailable: 1. Validate the configuration using load testing tools like k6 or hey while monitoring HPA scaling behavior and pod health metrics, and rollback via kubectl rollout undo deployment/image-processor if issues arise.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assesses practical CKAD mastery of production-ready deployments, autoscaling configuration, and operational safety measures in Kubernetes environments.

## Key Concepts

- Resource requests/limits for predictable performance and resource allocation
- Readiness and liveness probes for health monitoring and traffic management
- HorizontalPodAutoscaler for dynamic scaling based on CPU utilization
- PodDisruptionBudget for availability guarantees during maintenance
- RollingUpdate strategy for controlled deployments with zero downtime

## Code Example

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "100m"
    memory: "128Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 15
  periodSeconds: 20
```

```yaml
# HPA Configuration
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: image-processor-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: image-processor
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 75
```

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to compose CKAD primitives: ConfigMaps for runtime knobs, Secrets for credentials, InitContainers for bootstrapping assets, PVCs for content, and NetworkPolicy for egress control, all in a per-namespace scope.

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1

          claimName: site-pvc
      # ... Secrets/ConfigMaps mounted as envs or files
```

questions.question: CKAD intermediate: In namespace prod, Deployment payments-api with 4 replicas uses a vendor-base image that runs as root. Provide a minimal patch to enforce a strict security posture: container securityContext (runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities: drop: ['ALL']); PodSecurityContext (fsGroup: 2000); and include imagePullSecrets if the registry is private. Explain how you would validate under load and rollback if issues arise?
questions.answer: Patch the Deployment payments-api in prod to add a pod securityContext and container securityContext enforcing non-root operation, read-only root FS, and dropped capabilities; e.g.: RunAsNonRoot: true
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This checks practical hardening and CKAD adherence under real workflows, not theory. It spans securityContext, PodSecurityContext, imagePullSecrets, and rollback strategies.

## Key Concepts
- PodSecurityContext and containerSecurityContext
- runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities
- imagePullSecrets and rollout rollback
- Load testing and safe rollback procedures

## Code Example
```yaml
# patch example (conceptual)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments-api
  namespace: prod
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      containers:
      - name: payments-api
        image: vendor/payments-api:latest
        securityContext:
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          capabilities:
            drop: ['ALL']
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt if the vendor image requires a non-root user different from 1000?
- What metrics and rollback threshold would you use to safely roll this out across clusters?
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Identify risk] --> B[Apply patch]
  B --> C[Validate in staging]
  C --> D[Rollout to prod]
  D --> E[Monitor & rollback if issues]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical CKAD debugging: handling slow-start upgrades with probes, init containers for bootstrapping state, and PD&B safeguards to prevent downtime during maintenance.

## Key Concepts

- startupProbe and readinessProbe tuning
- initContainer for pre-warming caches
- PodDisruptionBudget for upgrade safety
- RollingUpdate strategy and rollback procedures

## Code Example

```yaml
# Minimal patch sketch (Deployment snippet)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: invoice-processor
  namespace: payments
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: init-cache
        image: invoice-processor:2.1
        command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /config/* /var/cache/ || true"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /config
        - name: cache
          mountPath: /var/cache
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: invoice-processor:2.1
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
      - name: config
        secret:
          secretName: app-secret
      - name: cache
        emptyDir: {}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you adapt this for a cluster with limited CPU headroom?
- What metrics would you monitor to confirm startup health and cache warmth?

questions.explanation:
Why This Is Asked
- Tests ability to wire ConfigMaps and Secrets across a namespace, plus InitContainer usage and lifecycle hooks.
- Evaluates observability via a metrics endpoint and logs on a PVC, with a simple autoscale path via HPA.
- Probes, resource requests, and minimal skeletons show readiness for real-world CKAD constraints.

Key Concepts
- ConfigMap/Secret integration, InitContainer tooling, PVC-backed logs, custom metrics exposure, readiness/liveness, HPA kinship, minimal YAML.

Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: feature-tester
  namespace: test-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: feature-tester
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: feature-tester
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: init-tools
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "apk add --no-cache httpie jq && mkdir -p /logs"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
      containers:
      - name: tester
        image: alpine:3.18
        command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do for url in $(cat /etc/feature-endpoints/endpoints); do resp=$(http --ignore-stdin GET "$url" Authorization:"Bearer $(cat /secret/api_token)"); echo "$url -> $resp" >> /logs/$(date +%s).log; done; sleep 60; done"]
        env:
        - name: ENDPOINTS_CONFIG
          value: "/etc/feature-endpoints/endpoints"
        - name: TOKEN_SECRET
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: api-token
              key: token
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
          name: metrics
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "256Mi"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /logs
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: feature-tester-logs
      - name: feature-endpoints
        configMap:
          name: feature-endpoints
      - name: secret
        secret:
          secretName: api-token
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: regcred
  
# Note: This is a minimal skeleton; real manifests should separate concerns (InitContainer, metrics, status storage) and handle errors gracefully.
```

questions.question: CKAD intermediate: In namespace inventory, a Deployment stock-service with 3 replicas mounts an emptyDir at /data and relies on a ConfigMap stock-config and a Secret db-credentials. Implement an InitContainer that waits for a Postgres DB at postgresql.inventory.svc:5432 and seeds data from /seed/init.sql (mounted from a ConfigMap). questions.answer: Add an InitContainer named seed-db to the stock-service Deployment that waits for postgresql.inventory.svc:5432 and runs psql -f /seed/init.sql using credentials from Secret db-credentials; mount /see
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to coordinate InitContainers with main containers, use ConfigMap/Secret mounted data, and implement health checks for reliable startups.

## Key Concepts
- InitContainers sequencing
- ConfigMap/Secret mounts
- Probes for startup health
- Rollback and load testing

## Code Example
```yaml
# fragment patch adding initContainer and volumes
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: stock-service
  namespace: inventory
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: seed-db
        image: postgres:15
        command:
        - bash
        - -lc
        - until pg_isready -h postgresql.inventory.svc -p 5432; do sleep 2; done; psql -h postgresql.inventory.svc -U dbuser -d inventory -f /seed/init.sql
        env:
        - name: PGPASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
        volumeMounts:
        - name: seed-files
          mountPath: /seed
      containers:
      - name: stock-service
        image: my-reg/stock-service:latest
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
      volumes:
      - name: seed-files
        configMap:
          name: stock-seed-config
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you scale this pattern for multiple environments?

## Why This Is Asked
Tests secret mounting, env wiring, and health checks for TLS in a CKAD context.

## Key Concepts
- Secret as files
- Env vars from paths
- Readiness checks for TLS paths
- Rollout validation and rollback criteria

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments

spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: payments
        image: your-image
        env:
        - name: TLS_CERT_FILE
          value: /tls/tls.crt
        - name: TLS_KEY_FILE

          secretName: tls-secret
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate zero-downtime during rollout with TLS cert changes?
- How would you audit access to tls-secret in Pod logs?

questions.question: CKAD intermediate: In namespace observability, a Deployment collector with 3 replicas throttles CPU under load. Provide a minimal patch to enable autoscaling and safe upgrades: (1) patch the Deployment with resource requests/limits and health probes; (2) add an HPA for min 2, max 6 with targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60; (3) add a PodDisruptionBudget to keep at least 2 healthy pods during maintenance. Include patches and describe validation under load?
questions.answer: Patch Deployment: set resources (requests cpu: 200m, limits cpu: 500m) and health probes for /health. Add HPA: minReplicas: 2, maxReplicas: 6, targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60. Add PodDisruptionBudg
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical CKAD skills: autoscaling, robustness, and upgrade safety in real workloads.

## Key Concepts

- Deployment resources and probes
- HorizontalPodAutoscaler (autoscale on CPU)
- PodDisruptionBudget for maintenance safety
- Observability: kubectl top, metrics-server

## Code Example

```yaml
# Deployment patch
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: collector
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: collector
        image: collector:latest
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 15
```

```yaml
# HPA
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: collector-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: collector
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 6
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 60
```

```yaml
# PodDisruptionBudget
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: collector-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: collector
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you verify HPA behavior under a synthetic load step?
- What changes if workloads are bursty and you want per-pod CPU caps?

questions.question: Design a per-namespace canary-traffic tester that coordinates a controlled canary rollout for a single Deployment within the namespace. The test reads CANARY_TARGET_APP, CANARY_VERS, and TARGET_SVC from a Namespace ConfigMap and API_TOKEN from a Secret; an InitContainer installs a lightweight CLI (curl/hey). It should shift 5% of traffic every 2 minutes by updating a ConfigMap entry that tracks canary percentage, logs per-request latency to a PVC at /logs, and stops on full rollout or rollback. Include readiness/liveness probes, a CPU-based HPA, and provide a minimal YAML skeleton; discuss trade-offs?
questions.answer: Implement a per-namespace canary tester that increments canary_pct in a Namespace ConfigMap by 5% every 2 minutes, sourcing CANARY_TARGET_APP, CANARY_VERS, TARGET_SVC from the ConfigMap and API_TOKEN
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design namespace-scoped control loops with observable metrics and safe rollout semantics.

## Key Concepts

- Readiness/Liveness probes and CPU-based HPA for constrained environments
- PVC-backed logs for latency measurements and rollback decisions

## Code Example

```yaml
# Minimal YAML skeleton for the canary tester (Deployment)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: canary-tester
  namespace: my-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: canary-tester
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: canary-tester
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: init-deps
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh","-c","apk add --no-cache curl jq >/dev/null 2>&1"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
      containers:
        - name: tester
          image: alpine:3.18
          command: ["sh","-c","sleep infinity"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: logs
              mountPath: /logs
      volumes:
        - name: logs
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: canary-logs-pvc
```
